The ONS ensures the counter increments **exactly once per part entry**, regardless of how long the sensor is blocked. **Step 3 — Ladder Diagram (3 marks):** ``` RUNG 1: One-Shot on Sensor Input | I:0/3 (Photo Sensor) | |----[ ]-----------------[ONS]-------------------------( )--| | XIC Bit B3:0/1 OTE Storage Bit B3:0/1 | RUNG 2: Counter Increment | B3:0/1 (ONS Output) | |----[ ]------------------------------------------------(CU)--| | XIC CTU C5:0 | | Preset = 100 | RUNG 3: Counter Reset | I:0/4 (Reset PB) | |----[ ]------------------------------------------------(RES)-| | XIC CTU C5:0 | ``` **Step 4 — Operation:** The ONS detects each part crossing the sensor and produces a 1-scan pulse. This pulse triggers the CTU counter to increment by 1. After 100 parts, the counter's Done bit (C5:0/DN) can be used to stop the conveyor or trigger an alarm. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** > 1. **Confusing XIC and XIO polarities:** The #1 mistake. Always verify: *"Does continuity pass when the bit is 1 or 0?"* > 2. **Using OTE where OTL is needed:** OTE is non-retentive; if a hold function is required, OTL/OTU MUST be used. > 3. **Forgetting the seal-in / auxiliary contact in motor latching logic:** A pure OTL/OTU pair works, but examiners often want the **classic three-element start-stop with a holding contact** as well. > 4. **Incorrect address format:** KTU accepts both AB-style (`I:0/0`) and Siemens-style (`I0.0`). Stay consistent within one answer. > 5. **One-Shot without a storage bit:** In RSLogix 500, ONS *requires* a tag (storage bit) for the ONS instruction to track the previous state. Forgetting the `B3:0/1` tag will lose you 1–2 marks. > 6. **In Boolean conversion, using OR instead of AND for series contacts:** Series contacts = AND. Parallel rungs = OR. This is non-negotiable. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **XIC (`\|---[ ]---\|`) passes logic when bit = 1.** It is a Normally Open contact. - [x] **XIO (`\|---[/]---\|`) passes logic when bit = 0.** It is a Normally Closed contact. - [x] **OTE (`\|---( )---\|`) is non-retentive** — output follows the rung in real time. - [x] **OTL / OTU are retentive** — output holds its state (Set/Reset flip-flop). - [x] **Series contacts = Boolean AND; Parallel rungs = Boolean OR.** - [x] **OTS / ONS produces a 1-scan pulse on a rising edge** — essential for debounce and counting. - [x] **E-Stops are ALWAYS programmed as XIO** for fail-safe operation. - [x] **PLC Scan Cycle order:** Read Inputs → Execute Program (rung-by-rung) → Write Outputs → Housekeeping. - [x] **Truth table for any On/Off instruction can be derived from the single equation** $C = B$ (XIC) or $C = \overline{B}$ (XIO). - [x] **Latching Equation (memorize):** $M_{new} = (S) \lor (M_{old} \cdot \overline{R})$ where S = Set rung, R = Reset rung. - [x] **One-Shot pulse width = 1 × $T_{scan}$** (e.g., 10 ms if scan time is 10 ms). - [x] **KTU typical platforms:** Allen-Bradley MicroLogix (AB syntax) and Siemens S7-1200 (Siemens syntax) — know both notations.

topics.title: Internal Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Relay (Auxiliary Relay / Marker Relay):** An *internal relay* is a **virtual, software-implemented relay** that resides entirely within the **Programmable Logic Controller's (PLC) central processing unit (CPU) memory**. It has **no physical output contact** connected to the external world. It functions as a **single-bit memory element** (boolean flag) that can be set (energized) or reset (de-energized) by the logic program and whose state can be read by any other rung in the program.

In standard IEC 61131-3 / manufacturer-specific nomenclature, internal relays are typically addressed using the **Memory (M) area** or **Marker (M) area** of the PLC. For example:

| Manufacturer | Address Notation | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Allen-Bradley (Rockwell) | `B3:0/0` (Binary file) | `B3:0/5` |
| Siemens (S7-1200/1500) | `M0.0` (Merker) | `M5.3` |
| Mitsubishi (MELSEC) | `M0` (Internal Relay) | `M100` |
| Schneider (Modicon) | `%M0` | `%MW10` |
| Generic IEC 61131-3 | `MX0.0` | `MX0.7` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Internal relays are addressed as **M (Memory) bits** in the PLC's memory map. Each internal relay consumes exactly **1 bit** of RAM and can have an **unlimited number of internal contacts** (NO/NC) referenced throughout the program.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **The "Whiteboard Marker" Analogy:** Imagine you are a factory shift supervisor writing tasks on a **whiteboard** in your office. The whiteboard marker is not connected to any machine outside your office — it is purely an **internal tool for your own thought process**. You might write "*Tank 1 is FULL*" on the board so that 10 different workers, who are each performing different sub-tasks, can all glance at the board and act on that same piece of information. 
> 
> An **internal relay works exactly the same way**:
> - The **whiteboard marker** = the internal relay coil (an output instruction in the PLC program).
> - The **writing on the board** = the 1-bit memory location in the CPU.
> - The **workers reading the board** = any number of `NO`/`NC` contact instructions elsewhere in the ladder program that "read" that bit.
> - The **whiteboard itself is inside the office** = the relay has NO physical terminal on the PLC output module. It exists *only* in CPU memory.

## 1.3 Why Internal Relays Are Indispensable in PLC Programming

A common student misconception is: *"Why not just use a physical output relay for everything?"* The reasons are:

1. **Conservation of physical I/O:** Physical outputs are expensive and limited (a 16-point output module can drive only 16 loads). Internal relays provide **unlimited auxiliary logic** without consuming hardware.
2. **Logical decomposition:** Complex Boolean expressions are broken into manageable sub-equations using internal relays as intermediate variables.
3. **Interlocking & Sequencing:** Multi-step industrial sequences (e.g., traffic lights, conveyor sorting) require state-holding elements between steps.
4. **Signal Conditioning:** Debouncing, edge detection, and pulse generation rely on internal flags.

> [!WARNING]
> **Crucial Distinction:** An **internal relay** is *not* the same as a **physical relay coil** in the output module. Setting internal relay `M0` does **NOT** turn on any external light, motor, or solenoid. It only flips a bit in the PLC's RAM.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

The following standard metrics govern internal relay implementation in any IEC 61131-3 compliant PLC:

- **Memory Footprint:** **1 bit** of volatile (or optionally retentive) CMOS RAM per relay.
- **Scan-Time Contribution:** Each internal relay contact reference adds **~0.1 µs to 2 µs** of scan-time overhead (manufacturer dependent).
- **Standard Capacity:** Modern PLCs typically provide **2048 to 8192** internal relay bits in the `M` area.
- **Retention Class:**
  - **Non-retentive (volatile):** State is cleared to `0` on power-down or STOP-to-RUN transition. Default class.
  - **Retentive (non-volatile):** State is preserved across power cycles using battery-backed SRAM or NVRAM. Must be explicitly configured in the PLC's hardware configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How an Internal Relay Works

The internal relay mechanism operates on a **two-instruction primitive** in ladder logic:

### 2.1.1 The Coil (Output) Instruction
A coil instruction, when energized by a `TRUE` (passing power) rung, performs a **bit-write** to a memory location.

| Coil Type | Symbol | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Coil** `-( )-` | Round parentheses | Sets bit = `1` if rung is TRUE; resets to `0` if rung is FALSE |
| **Set (Latch) Coil** `-(S)-` | "S" inside parentheses | Sets bit = `1` (sticky); remains `1` even if rung goes FALSE |
| **Reset (Unlatch) Coil** `-(R)-` | "R" inside parentheses | Resets bit = `0`; remains `0` even if rung goes TRUE |
| **Negated Coil** `-(/)-` | Slash inside parentheses | Sets bit = `0` if rung is TRUE (inverted assignment) |

### 2.1.2 The Contact (Input) Instruction
A contact instruction performs a **bit-read** of a memory location. The bit is *read* — never modified — so an unlimited number of contacts can reference the same bit.

| Contact Type | Symbol | Passes Power When... |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normally Open (NO / Examine-On)** `-\|-` | Two vertical bars | The referenced bit = `1` |
| **Normally Closed (NC / Examine-Off)** `-/-\|` | Two vertical bars with a slash | The referenced bit = `0` |
| **Positive Edge (One-Shot Rising)** `-\|↑\|-` | "P" or up-arrow | Bit transitions from `0` → `1` (single scan TRUE) |
| **Negative Edge (One-Shot Falling)** `-\|↓\|-` | "N" or down-arrow | Bit transitions from `1` → `0` (single scan TRUE) |

## 2.2 The Logic Behind Each Step

1. **Bit Allocation:** When the PLC project is compiled, the programmer (or automatic address allocator) assigns each `M` symbol a unique offset in the `M` memory word file (e.g., `M0.0` to `M0.7` occupy the 8 bits of word `MW0`).
2. **Scan-Time Evaluation:** During the **Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan** cycle (the standard PLC scan), the CPU evaluates every rung sequentially.
3. **Coil Write-Through:** When a coil instruction `-( M0.0 )-` is encountered with a TRUE logic result, the CPU **writes `1`** to the designated bit. The write takes effect **immediately** within the same scan — so any contact instruction *below* the same rung will see the updated value. (This is the classic PLC "last-writer-wins" semantics, unlike traditional electrical wiring where relays are passive.)
4. **Contact Read-Only:** A contact `-| M0.0 |-` only *queries* the bit; it never modifies it. This means **a single internal relay can be wired (in ladder logic) to appear in thousands of rungs** without conflict.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Read-After-Write" Rule in a Single Scan:** Within one scan cycle, if a rung sets `M0.0 = 1`, all rungs *after* it (in execution order) will see `M0.0 = 1`. Rungs *before* it will see the *old* value of `M0.0` from the previous scan.

## 2.3 Retentive vs. Non-Retentive Internal Relays

| Property | Non-Retentive Internal Relay | Retentive Internal Relay |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memory Type** | Volatile CMOS RAM | Battery-backed SRAM or NVRAM |
| **State on Power Loss** | Reset to `0` | Preserved (last state held) |
| **State on STOP→RUN** | Reset to `0` (typically) | Preserved |
| **Use Case** | Transient conditions, flags, sequences that must restart on power-up | Process counters, recipe data, fault latches |
| **Typical Address** | `M0.0` (default) | `MB0` in Siemens, separate retentive `MR` file in AB |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **engineering metrics and Boolean identities** that govern internal relay usage. These are the high-yield items for KTU 2024 numerical and analytical questions.

| Parameter / Identity | Expression / Value | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memory per internal relay** | $1 \text{ bit} = \frac{1}{8} \text{ byte}$ | RAM bytes |
| **Total internal relays in a word** | $1 \text{ word} = 16 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 16 \text{ relays}$ | Standard PLC word |
| **Scan-time overhead per contact** | $\Delta t_{\text{scan}} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 2 \; \mu\text{s}$ | Microseconds |
| **Boolean AND via series contacts** | $A \cdot B = C$ (relay C set when both A and B are TRUE) | Logic identity |
| **Boolean OR via parallel contacts** | $A + B = C$ (relay C set when either A or B is TRUE) | Logic identity |
| **De Morgan's Law (relay logic)** | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | Inversion via NC contacts |
| **Edge-Detection (Rising)** | $M_{\text{pulse}} = A \cdot \overline{A_{\text{prev}}}$ | One-scan TRUE pulse |
| **Latch Set/Reset Equation** | $M = (S + M) \cdot \overline{R}$ | Dominant-reset latch |
| **Maximum addressable internal relays (typical)** | $N_{\text{relays}} = 2^{16} = 65\,536$ | For 16-bit word addressing |
| **Retention battery life (typical)** | $T_{\text{batt}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 10 \text{ years}$ | Lithium backup cell |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Internal relays are the **"glue" of every industrial PLC program** in the world. Concrete industry applications include:

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Tracking the position of a car body on a conveyor using a sequence of internal flags (`M0`=at station 1, `M1`=at station 2, etc.).
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Latching an "over-pressure" fault flag that requires manual reset by an operator.
- **Packaging Machinery:** Edge-detection flags to count products passing a photoelectric sensor.
- **HVAC Control:** Sequencing chillers, pumps, and cooling towers with a state-machine implemented in internal relays.
- **Traffic Light Controllers:** Multi-state sequence (Red → Green → Yellow) using internal flags as state registers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Boolean Logic Implementation Using Internal Relays

**Problem:** Realize the Boolean function $F = A \cdot B + \overline{C} \cdot D$ using a single output physical relay (call it `Y0`) and an arbitrary number of internal relays (`M0`, `M1`).

**Step 1 — Identify Sub-Expressions:**
The function has two product terms: $T_1 = A \cdot B$ and $T_2 = \overline{C} \cdot D$. 
We will assign one internal relay to each product term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= A \cdot B \quad \text{(AND of inputs A and B)} \\
M_1 &= \overline{C} \cdot D \quad \text{(AND of NOT-C with D)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Final Output:**
The output $Y_0$ is the OR of the two product terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 &= M_0 + M_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Sub-Expressions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 &= (A \cdot B) + (\overline{C} \cdot D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ladder Logic Realization (3 Rungs):**
- **Rung 1:** `A` (NO) — `B` (NO) → coil `M0`. Rung logic: power flows only if both A and B are TRUE.
- **Rung 2:** `C` (NC) — `D` (NO) → coil `M1`. Rung logic: power flows only if C is FALSE and D is TRUE.
- **Rung 3:** `M0` (NO) parallel with `M1` (NO) → coil `Y0` (physical output).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this decomposition matters:** Without internal relays `M0` and `M1`, the original function $F = A \cdot B + \overline{C} \cdot D$ would require a single rung with 4 series-parallel contacts. Such a rung is **hard to read, debug, and modify** in the field. By introducing intermediate flags, we create a **structured, modular program**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Latching Internal Relay (Bistable Memory Element)

**Problem:** A conveyor motor must be **started by a momentary "Start" pushbutton (S)** and **stopped by a momentary "Stop" pushbutton (X)**. The motor should remain running even after the Start button is released. Implement using an internal relay `M0` (which will then drive the physical output `Y0`).

**Step 1 — Identify the Latch Equation:**
A standard dominant-reset latch has the Boolean equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (S + M_0) \cdot \overline{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $M_0$ becomes `1` if either the Start button `S` is pressed OR $M_0$ is already `1` (the "hold" path), **AND** the Stop button `X` is NOT pressed.

**Step 2 — Construct the Ladder Rung:**
- Place `S` (NO contact of Start) **in parallel** with `M0` (NO contact of the internal relay itself).
- Place this parallel combination **in series** with `X` (NC contact of Stop).
- Drive the coil `M0` with the result.

```
   ┌──[ S ]──┐
   │         │
───┤         ├──[ /X ]──────────( M0 )──
   │         │
   └──[ M0 ]─┘
```

**Step 3 — Truth Table Verification:**

| Pre-state $M_0$ | Start `S` | Stop `X` | New $M_0$ | Explanation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Idle state — nothing pressed |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **1** | Start pressed → $M_0$ latches ON |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | **1** | Self-holding via the `M0` contact |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | **1** | Start re-pressed — no change (already ON) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **0** | Stop pressed → latch broken |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **0** | Stop has priority (dominant reset) |

**Step 4 — Drive the Physical Output:**
A separate rung energizes the motor contactor: `--[ M0 ]---------( Y0 )--`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Best Practice:** It is common practice to use an **internal relay for the latching logic** and then assign the **physical output** to a separate rung driven by that relay. This isolates the logic from the I/O assignment, allowing easy re-mapping if the hardware configuration changes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — One-Shot (Edge-Detection) Internal Relay

**Problem:** Generate a single-scan TRUE pulse when a normally-open limit switch `LS1` transitions from OFF to ON. This pulse is used to increment a counter by exactly **one count per actuation**, regardless of how long `LS1` stays closed.

**Step 1 — Define the Edge-Detection Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{pulse}} &= LS_1 \cdot \overline{LS_1^{\text{prev}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $LS_1^{\text{prev}}$ is the value of `LS1` from the **previous scan cycle**.

**Step 2 — Implement Using Two Internal Relays:**
- `M0` = current-state buffer of `LS1`.
- `M1` = edge-detection flag (the one-shot pulse).

**Step 3 — Ladder Logic (3 Rungs):**

```
--[ LS1 ]-----------------------------( M0 )--    // Rung 1: buffer the input

--[ M0 ]--[/ M0_prev ]-----------------( M1 )--   // Rung 2: rising edge
                                  (assume M0_prev is the value from last scan)
--[ M1 ]--[ CNT C5:0 ]  ... (counter increment instruction)  
```

**Step 4 — Quantitative Analysis (Why One Scan Only):**
At scan $n$, suppose `LS1` transitions from `0` to `1`.
- `M0` (the buffer) was `0` last scan and becomes `1` this scan.
- `M1` evaluates as: $M0_{\text{current}} \cdot \overline{M0_{\text{previous}}} = 1 \cdot \overline{0} = 1$.
- $M1 = 1$ for **exactly one scan** (the scan where the transition occurred).
- At scan $n+1$, $M0_{\text{previous}} = 1$, so $M1 = 1 \cdot \overline{1} = 0$ — the pulse ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Most modern PLCs provide built-in **Positive Differential (PD)** or **One-Shot Rising (OSR)** instructions that perform this function automatically in a single rung. The internal-relay-based derivation above is the **conceptual foundation** for understanding how those built-in instructions work internally.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Logic Functions to Internal Relay Patterns

| Desired Logic Operation | Number of Internal Relays Used | Typical Ladder Pattern |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 2-input AND ($A \cdot B$) | 1 | Series NO contacts → coil |
| 2-input OR ($A + B$) | 1 | Parallel NO contacts → coil |
| 3-input NAND ($\overline{A \cdot B \cdot C}$) | 1 | Series NO contacts → negated coil `-(/)-` |
| 3-input NOR ($\overline{A + B + C}$) | 1 | Parallel NO contacts → negated coil |
| XOR ($A \oplus B$) | 2 | $M_0 = A \cdot \overline{B}$; $M_1 = \overline{A} \cdot B$; $Y = M_0 + M_1$ |
| Latch (Set/Reset) | 1 (or 2) | Self-holding parallel branch + reset series branch |
| Edge Detection | 2 | Buffer + AND-with-previous-NOT |
| Multi-State Sequencer | $N$ flags (one per state) | Mutually-exclusive flag pattern |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Internal Relay Memory Organization

The following block-level architecture maps how an internal relay is allocated, written, and read within the PLC's CPU memory hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[USER LADDER PROGRAM] --> B[COMPILER ADDRESS RESOLVER]
    B --> C[MEMORY MAP FILE M AREA]
    C --> D1[WORD MW0 BITS M0.0 M0.1 ... M0.15]
    C --> D2[WORD MW1 BITS M1.0 M1.1 ... M1.15]
    C --> D3[WORD MW2 BITS M2.0 M2.1 ... M2.15]
    C --> D4[DOTS CONTINUATION UP TO MWn]
    D1 --> E1[COIL INSTRUCTION WRITE PORT]
    D1 --> F1[CONTACT INSTRUCTION READ PORT 1]
    D1 --> F2[CONTACT INSTRUCTION READ PORT 2]
    D1 --> F3[CONTACT INSTRUCTION READ PORT N]
    E1 --> G[RUNG EVALUATION ENGINE]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[PHYSICAL OUTPUT MODULE Y AREA]
    G -.NO CONNECTION.-> I[EXTERNAL FIELD DEVICES]
    style A fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5F9EA0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **dashed line** from the "Rung Evaluation Engine" to "External Field Devices" emphasizes that an internal relay has **no physical output path** — the connection is deliberately broken. In contrast, the "Physical Output Module" (`Y` area) has a **solid line** to the field devices.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Flow — Internal Relay Latch Operation

This flow diagram illustrates the complete state-transition behavior of a latched internal relay driving a conveyor motor.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    S1[IDLE M0 EQUALS 0 MOTOR OFF] -->|START S PRESSED| S2[HOLDING M0 EQUALS 1 MOTOR ON]
    S2 -->|START RELEASED| S3[SELF HOLDING M0 STAYS 1]
    S3 -->|STOP X PRESSED| S4[RESET M0 EQUALS 0 MOTOR OFF]
    S4 -->|STOP RELEASED| S1
    S2 -->|STOP X PRESSED| S4
    S3 -->|POWER CYCLE NON RETENTIVE| S1
    S3 -->|POWER CYCLE RETENTIVE| S2
    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Wiring vs. Ladder Logic — The Paradigm Shift

To reinforce that internal relays are *purely virtual*, the following block diagram contrasts the **old electromagnetic relay panel** with the **modern PLC-based equivalent**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PANEL[TRADITIONAL ELECTROMAGNETIC RELAY PANEL]
        A1[FIELD INPUT DEVICES PUSHBUTTONS SENSORS] -->|WIRES| A2[COIL RELAY K1 K2 K3 K4]
        A2 -->|WIRES| A3[CONTACTS K1 K2 K3 K4]
        A3 -->|WIRES| A4[FIELD OUTPUTS MOTORS SOLENOIDS]
    end
    subgraph PLCV[MODERN PLC VIRTUAL EQUIVALENT]
        B1[INPUT MODULE I AREA] -->|INTERNAL BUS| B2[CPU MEMORY M AREA INTERNAL RELAYS M0 M1 M2 M3]
        B2 -->|INTERNAL BUS| B3[CPU LADDER LOGIC EVALUATION]
        B3 -->|INTERNAL BUS| B4[OUTPUT MODULE Y AREA]
    end
    style A2 fill:#CD853F,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway from the diagram:** The physical wiring between the relay coil and its contacts in the traditional panel is replaced by the **internal CPU memory bus** in the PLC. This is why we say an internal relay "lives" inside the CPU and has no physical terminal.

---

## 4.4 Counter Implementation Using Internal Relays

A simple up-counter implemented with internal relays (conceptual view; modern PLCs use dedicated `CTU` / `CNT` instructions, but the principle is the same).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[PULSE INPUT FROM SENSOR] --> C1[INTERNAL RELAY M0 EDGE DETECTOR]
    C1 --> C2[INTERNAL RELAY M1 ACCUMULATOR FLAG]
    C2 -->|COUNT EQUALS 10| C3[INTERNAL RELAY M2 PRESET REACHED]
    C3 --> OUT[PHYSICAL OUTPUT Y0 DUMP CONVEYOR]
    C3 -->|RESET FROM HMI BUTTON| RST[M3 RESET FLAG]
    RST --> C2
    style P1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RST fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Internal Relay" in a PLC system. How is it addressed in typical manufacturer-specific memory maps?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An internal relay is a **virtual, software-implemented single-bit memory element** within the PLC's CPU. It has **no physical output contact** and exists solely for use within the ladder program. 

Addressing examples:
- **Allen-Bradley:** `B3:0/0` (Binary file B3, word 0, bit 0).
- **Siemens:** `M0.0` (Merker byte 0, bit 0).
- **Mitsubishi:** `M0` (Internal relay 0).

Each internal relay occupies **1 bit of RAM**, and an unlimited number of internal contacts (NO/NC) can be programmed to read the same bit. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between retentive and non-retentive internal relays. State one industrial application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Non-Retentive | Retentive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory | Volatile RAM | Battery-backed / NVRAM |
| State after power loss | Reset to 0 | Preserved |

- **Non-retentive example:** A "cycle-in-progress" flag in a packaging machine that should reset on every power-up for safety. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Retentive example:** A "total products manufactured" counter or a fault-alarm latch that must survive a power outage for maintenance review. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the following applications of internal relays in PLC programming. For each, sketch the ladder logic and state the Boolean equation.
**(i) Latching (memory) circuit using a single internal relay.**
**(ii) Interlocking between two outputs (e.g., Motor Forward and Motor Reverse).**
**(iii) Edge-detection / one-shot pulse generation.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Latching Circuit:**
- The relay `M0` is wired in parallel with its own NO contact to create a self-holding (seal-in) path.
- The Stop pushbutton `X` (NC contact) is in series with the parallel combination.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (S + M_0) \cdot \overline{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

```
   ┌──[ S ]──┐
   │         │
───┤         ├──[ /X ]──────────( M0 )──
   │         │
   └──[ M0 ]─┘
```

**(2 marks — equation + ladder + explanation of self-holding)**

**(ii) Interlocking Circuit:**
- Two internal flags `M0` (Forward request) and `M1` (Reverse request) are used to drive outputs `Y0` (Forward contactor) and `Y1` (Reverse contactor).
- A normally-closed contact of `M0` is placed in series with the rung driving `Y1`, and a normally-closed contact of `M1` is placed in series with the rung driving `Y0`.
- This guarantees that **both contactors can never be energized simultaneously**, preventing a phase-to-phase short circuit in the motor.

```
--[ FWD_PB ]--[/ M1 ]-----------( M0 )--
--[ REV_PB ]--[/ M0 ]-----------( M1 )--
--[ M0 ]-----------------------( Y0 )--  (Forward contactor)
--[ M1 ]-----------------------( Y1 )--  (Reverse contactor)
```

Boolean equations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 &= M_0 \cdot \overline{M_1} \\
Y_1 &= M_1 \cdot \overline{M_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks — interlocking concept, ladder, equations)**

**(iii) Edge Detection:**
- Two internal relays are used: `M0` buffers the current state of the input `IN`, and `M1` generates a single-scan pulse on the rising edge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= IN \\
M_1 &= M_0 \cdot \overline{M_0_{\text{prev}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Application:** Counting products on a conveyor — the counter should increment **once per object**, not continuously while the object is in front of the sensor. **(2 marks — explanation + application)**

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A chemical mixing tank has two inlet valves (V1, V2) and one outlet valve (V3). V1 and V2 open when their respective start buttons (S1, S2) are pressed and must remain open until the corresponding stop buttons (X1, X2) are pressed. V3 opens automatically when a level switch (L) detects that the tank is FULL and closes when the level drops to LOW. Design the PLC program using internal relays. Show all rungs, label every contact, and write the Boolean equations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Allocate Internal Relays:**
- `M0` = Latch for V1
- `M1` = Latch for V2
- `M2` = "Tank Full" status (driven directly by L, but mirrored here for clarity)

**Step 2 — Boolean Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (S_1 + M_0) \cdot \overline{X_1} \\
M_1 &= (S_2 + M_1) \cdot \overline{X_2} \\
M_2 &= L \\
Y_1 &= M_0 \quad \text{(Physical output: Valve 1)} \\
Y_2 &= M_1 \quad \text{(Physical output: Valve 2)} \\
Y_3 &= L \cdot \overline{\text{LOW}} \quad \text{(V3 opens on FULL, closes on LOW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram:**

```
Rung 1:  ──[ S1 ]──┬──[ /X1 ]──────────( M0 )──
                  │
                  └──[ M0 ]──┘
Rung 2:  ──[ S2 ]──┬──[ /X2 ]──────────( M1 )──
                  │
                  └──[ M1 ]──┘
Rung 3:  ──[ L ]──────────────────────────( M2 )──
Rung 4:  ──[ M0 ]─────────────────────────( Y1 )──
Rung 5:  ──[ M1 ]─────────────────────────( Y2 )──
Rung 6:  ──[ L ]──[ /LOW ]────────────────( Y3 )──
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of Rung 6:**
The V3 outlet valve opens when the level switch `L` indicates the tank is full. It remains open until a second level switch (LOW) detects the tank has emptied, at which point the NC contact `[ /LOW ]` opens and de-energizes `Y3`. **(2 marks for the logic decomposition and the use of internal relays to isolate latching from output assignment)**

**[Total: 7 marks — 1 for relay allocation, 2 for equations, 2 for ladder, 1 for V3 logic, 1 for explanation]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

#### (a) [7 marks] With the help of neat ladder diagrams, explain how internal relays are used to implement the following:
**(i) A 3-input AND function $F = A \cdot B \cdot C$**
**(ii) A 2-input XOR function $F = A \oplus B$**
**(iii) A dominant-set SR latch (set has priority over reset)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) 3-Input AND:** A single internal relay `M0` is energized by three series NO contacts.

```
──[ A ]──[ B ]──[ C ]────────( M0 )──
Y0 = M0
```

Boolean: $M_0 = A \cdot B \cdot C$, then $F = M_0$. **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) 2-input XOR:** Two internal relays decompose the XOR into two AND terms and one OR sum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \oplus B = (A \cdot \overline{B}) + (\overline{A} \cdot B) \\
M_0 &= A \cdot \overline{B} \\
M_1 &= \overline{A} \cdot B \\
F &= M_0 + M_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Ladder:
```
Rung 1: ──[ A ]──[ /B ]──────────────( M0 )──
Rung 2: ──[ /A ]──[ B ]──────────────( M1 )──
Rung 3: ──[ M0 ]──┐
                   ├──( F )──
         ──[ M1 ]──┘
```

**(2.5 marks — derivation + ladder + verification)**

**(iii) Dominant-Set SR Latch:** When both Set and Reset are asserted simultaneously, the Set input wins.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (S + M_0) \cdot \overline{R \cdot \overline{S}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent: $M_0 = S + (M_0 \cdot \overline{R})$

Ladder (using two rungs):
```
Rung 1: ──[ S ]──┬─────────────────( M0 )──
                 │
                 └──[ M0 ]──[ /R ]──┘
Rung 2: ──[ S ]──[ R ]──[/ M0 ]──( M0 )──    // Force ON when both asserted
```

**Application:** Emergency-stop circuits where pressing the E-Stop must **always** override any running condition. **(3 marks — equation, ladder, priority discussion)**

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A conveyor belt system has the following requirements:
- A green "RUN" lamp must be ON whenever the conveyor motor is running.
- A red "FAULT" lamp must latch ON whenever the motor thermal overload relay (OL) trips, and must remain latched even if the overload cools down.
- A yellow "START DELAY" lamp must glow for exactly **5 seconds** after the Start button is pressed, regardless of the motor's state.
- A RESET pushbutton must clear the FAULT latch.

Design the PLC program using internal relays, timer bits, and output assignments. Show the ladder logic, the Boolean equations, and a brief description of each rung.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Allocation:**
- `M0` = Latched "Conveyor Running" flag
- `M1` = Latched "Fault" flag (retentive recommended)
- `M2` = "Start Delay" timer-running flag
- `T1` = TON timer preset 5 s, done bit `T1.DN`

**Step 2 — Boolean Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (S + M_0) \cdot \overline{X} \cdot \overline{M_1} \quad \text{(Start latch, stopped by Stop X or Fault M1)} \\
M_1 &= (OL \cdot M_0) + M_1 \quad \text{(set by overload while running, sticky)} \\
\overline{M_1} &= \text{RESET} \quad \text{(Reset button unlatches fault)} \\
M_2 &= S \cdot \overline{T1.DN} \quad \text{(Start delay active for 5 s)} \\
Y_{\text{motor}} &= M_0 \\
Y_{\text{green}} &= M_0 \\
Y_{\text{red}} &= M_1 \\
Y_{\text{yellow}} &= M_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram (7 rungs):**

```
Rung 1: ──[ S ]──┬──[ /X ]──[ /M1 ]──( M0 )──
                  │
                  └──[ M0 ]──┘
Rung 2: ──[ M0 ]──[ OL ]─────────────( S M1 )──    // Set fault latch
Rung 3: ──[ RESET ]──────────────────( R M1 )──    // Reset fault latch
Rung 4: ──[ S ]──[ TON T1 K=50 ]────( T1 )──       // 5 s timer (K=50 × 0.1 s)
Rung 5: ──[ S ]──[ /T1.DN ]─────────( M2 )──       // Delay-active flag
Rung 6: ──[ M0 ]────────────────────( Ymotor )──
Rung 7: ──[ M0 ]────────────────────( Ygreen )──
Rung 8: ──[ M1 ]────────────────────( Yred )──
Rung 9: ──[ M2 ]────────────────────( Yellow )──
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of Key Rungs:**
- **Rung 1:** Self-holding start latch with **two stop conditions** — the manual Stop button `X` and the Fault flag `M1`. This means a fault automatically halts the conveyor.
- **Rung 2 & 3:** Classic **Set/Reset (SR) latch** using the Set and Reset coil instructions. The fault can only be cleared by the dedicated `RESET` button, satisfying the latching requirement.
- **Rung 4 & 5:** The TON (Timer-On-Delay) instruction `T1` accumulates time while `S` is TRUE. The done bit `T1.DN` becomes TRUE after 5 s, breaking the `M2` rung. Thus `M2` (yellow lamp) is TRUE for exactly 5 s after Start, regardless of motor state. **(1 mark for explaining timer behavior, 1 mark for output assignments)**

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `M` bits with physical outputs:** Students frequently write `Y0` and `M0` interchangeably, costing **1 to 2 marks**. Remember: `M` = internal (no physical terminal), `Y` = physical output module.
> 2. **Forgetting the self-holding contact:** In latching circuits, the NO contact of the relay **must be wired in parallel with the start button**. Omitting this is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Stop button contact type:** The Stop button in a latching circuit **must be a Normally-Closed (NC) contact in series** with the rung, not NO. Using NO is a fatal safety error and costs marks.
> 4. **Edge-detection logic reversal:** For a **rising-edge** pulse, the formula is $M_{\text{pulse}} = A \cdot \overline{A_{\text{prev}}}$. Students often write $A \cdot A_{\text{prev}}$, which is incorrect.
> 5. **Scan-order dependency:** A coil instruction affects rungs *below* it in the same scan, not *above*. Forgetting this leads to wrong timing diagrams and **2-mark loss**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Internal Relay"**

- ☐ **Definition:** Internal relay = virtual, 1-bit memory element inside CPU, **no physical output**.
- ☐ **Address notation:** `M0.0` (Siemens), `M0` (Mitsubishi), `B3:0/0` (Allen-Bradley), `%M0` (Schneider).
- ☐ **Memory footprint:** Exactly **1 bit** of RAM per internal relay.
- ☐ **Unlimited contacts:** Any number of NO/NC contacts can read the same `M` bit without conflict.
- ☐ **Two instruction types:** **Coil** (write: `-( )-`, `-(S)-`, `-(R)-`, `-(/)-`) and **Contact** (read: `-\|-`, `-/\|`, `-\|↑\|-`, `-\|↓\|-`).
- ☐ **Read-after-write rule:** Within a single scan, a coil write is visible to all rungs *below* it in execution order.
- ☐ **Non-retentive** = state lost on power-down (volatile RAM).
- ☐ **Retentive** = state preserved on power-down (battery-backed / NVRAM).
- ☐ **Latching equation:** $M = (S + M) \cdot \overline{R}$ — dominant-reset variant.
- ☐ **Dominant-set equation:** $M = S + (M \cdot \overline{R})$ — set has priority.
- ☐ **Edge detection (rising):** $M_{\text{pulse}} = A \cdot \overline{A_{\text{prev}}}$, lasts exactly **one scan**.
- ☐ **Interlocking pattern:** Use NC contacts of mutually-exclusive flags in series with the opposing rung's coil.
- ☐ **De Morgan applications:** Inverted coils `-(/)-` implement NAND/NOR efficiently.
- ☐ **Industry use cases:** Sequencing, interlocking, latching alarms, debouncing, state machines, edge-triggered counting, HMI handshake flags.
- ☐ **Key advantage:** Conserves physical I/O; enables structured, modular, debuggable programs.
- ☐ **KTU frequent keywords to use in answers:** "auxiliary relay", "marker", "virtual coil", "memory bit", "seal-in contact", "self-holding", "dominant reset", "retentive memory".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jump Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Jump Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Jump Instructions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jump Instruction:** A program control instruction in the PLC ladder logic that interrupts the **normal sequential scan execution** of the program (top-to-bottom, left-to-right) and forces the CPU's program counter to transfer control to a **predefined target label (LBL)** elsewhere in the program, either **unconditionally** or based on the **logical state of a precondition rung**.

In the IEC 61131-3 standard and in manufacturer-specific implementations (Allen-Bradley / Rockwell, Siemens S7, Mitsubishi, Schneider, Omron), jump instructions are classified as **program control / flow-control instructions**. They are essential for building compact, efficient, and logically structured PLC programs where portions of logic must be bypassed under certain operating conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Recipe Analogy:** Imagine you are following a printed recipe to cook dinner.
> - **Normal execution** = reading every step in order (boil water → add pasta → wait 10 min → drain).
> - **Unconditional jump (JMP)** = a sticky note that says "go directly to the dessert section, skip everything else."
> - **Conditional jump (CJP / JMPC)** = a sticky note that says "**IF** the guest is vegetarian, **THEN** jump to the salad section; otherwise continue with the meat recipe."
> - **Master Control Relay (MCR)** = a master switch that **de-energizes an entire zone** of the kitchen (all burners in that section go off, regardless of what the recipe says).
> - **Subroutine (JSR/SBR)** = a pointer that says "go read the sauce recipe in another cookbook, prepare the sauce, then come back to the exact step you left from."

This is exactly how a PLC uses jump instructions: it decides *which rungs* of the ladder program will be **scanned and executed** during a given CPU cycle, and which will be **skipped over** without execution.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

| Aspect | Role of Jump Instructions |
|---|---|
| **Scan Time Optimization** | Reduces cycle time by skipping non-relevant logic blocks. |
| **Operational Mode Selection** | Allows manual / auto / service mode switching. |
| **Fault Isolation** | Bypasses a faulty machine section without halting the entire line. |
| **Compact Coding** | Avoids duplicating large blocks of logic using enable bits. |
| **Safe Sequencing** | Implements interlock-driven start-up and shut-down sequences. |

The standard boundary metric for scan time in modular PLCs is often quoted as **10–20 ms per 1K instructions**, with jumps being one of the prime tools to keep this within spec.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Jump & Program-Control Instructions

The complete family of program-control instructions found across major PLC platforms is listed below.

#### 2.1.1 Unconditional Jump (JMP)
- Always transfers control to the target label when the rung is reached.
- No logical condition gates the transfer.
- Common in ladder logic sectioning (e.g., Allen-Bradley SLC-500 `JMP` and `LBL`).

#### 2.1.2 Conditional Jump (CJP / JMPC / JMP)
- Transfer occurs **only when the rung logic evaluates TRUE** (logic continuity).
- If the rung is FALSE, the jump is **not** executed; the CPU proceeds to the next sequential rung.

#### 2.1.3 Master Control Relay (MCR)
- A special zone-control instruction that **de-energizes all non-retentive (non-latched) outputs** within its bounded region when the controlling rung is FALSE.
- It does *not* skip rungs execution-wise; it forces outputs to OFF.

#### 2.1.4 Subroutine Call (JSR / SBR) and Return (RET)
- Transfers control to a **named subroutine file/block**, executes it, and **returns** to the instruction immediately following the call.
- Implements reusable, modular code.

#### 2.1.5 Immediate Input/Output Update (IIN / IOT)
- Forces an I/O update *mid-scan* (often paired with jumps for time-critical sections).

#### 2.1.6 Program End (END / ENDM / MEND)
- Marks the final executable instruction. In some platforms, an early `END` acts like an implicit jump to program termination.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

For a **conditional jump** instruction, the operational sequence in a single scan cycle is:

1. The CPU reaches the rung containing the JMP/CJP instruction.
2. The ladder logic to the left of the JMP coil is evaluated (contact network).
3. The boolean result is latched into the instruction's enable bit.
4. **If TRUE** → the program counter is loaded with the address of the matching `LBL`. The CPU skips all rungs in between.
5. **If FALSE** → the program counter is simply incremented; the JMP coil is treated as a NOP (no operation), and the next sequential rung is executed.
6. After executing the jumped-to block, the scan continues normally past the label.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Equation | Standard Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{scan}$ | Total PLC scan time | ms (milliseconds) |
| $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{logic} + T_{output} + T_{overhead}$ | Sum of input read, logic execution, output write, and housekeeping | $T_{overhead} \le 5\%$ of $T_{scan}$ |
| $T_{logic} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{exec}} T_{rung,i}$ | Sum of rung execution times for **executed** rungs | ms |
| $J_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{exec}}{N_{total}}$ | Jump effectiveness ratio (fraction of rungs executed) | Dimensionless, $0 \le J_{eff} \le 1$ |
| $PC_{next} = (J_{active} \cdot LBL_{addr}) + (1 - J_{active}) \cdot (PC_{curr}+1)$ | Program counter update rule | Integer memory address |
| $MCR_{out} = MCR_{en} \cdot (OUT_{retentive} \vee \overline{MCR_{en}})$ | Output state under MCR | Boolean |
| $N_{retentive}$ | Number of latched/retentive outputs unaffected by MCR | Count |
| $f_{scan}$ | Scan frequency $= 1 / T_{scan}$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner's Note:** The value $J_{eff}$ directly determines scan time savings. A well-designed program with a JMP often has $J_{eff} \le 0.5$, halving logic execution time for that section.

### 2.4 Manufacturer-Specific Implementations (Board-Relevant)

| Manufacturer | Jump Instruction | Label Instruction | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Allen-Bradley (RSLogix 500) | `JMP` | `LBL` | JMP and LBL share a numeric file number (0–255). |
| Allen-Bradley (Studio 5000) | `JMP` | `LBL` | Tag-based labels; supports nested jumps. |
| Siemens (S7-300/400, TIA Portal) | `JC` (cond.), `JCN` (uncond.) | Label is a `Network` number | `JC` jumps if RLO = 1. |
| Siemens S7-1200/1500 | `JMP` / `JMPN` | `Label:` | Structured text variants exist. |
| Mitsubishi (GX Works) | `CJ` / `SCJ` | `P` (pointer label) | `SCJ` = scan-execute type conditional jump. |
| Omron (CX-Programmer) | `JMP(04)` / `CJP(05)` | — | Different format from AB. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Assembly Line:** Skipping robotic weld sequences for a body style that does not require a particular weld station.
2. **Bottling Plant:** Bypassing a CIP (Clean-In-Place) routine when the line is in production mode.
3. **HVAC Systems:** Jumping over heating-coil logic when the system is in cooling-only mode.
4. **Packaging Machinery:** Using MCR to de-energize all conveyors during an E-stop, except for the latched alarm outputs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Scan Time Reduction Using a Jump

Consider a ladder program with $N_{total}$ rungs. Without any jump instruction, the CPU executes **all** rungs every scan:

$$
T_{logic,full} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} T_{rung,i}
$$

Assume the rung execution times are uniform: $T_{rung,i} = T_0$ for all $i$. Then:

$$
T_{logic,full} = N_{total} \cdot T_0
$$

Now introduce a **conditional jump** at rung $K$ that bypasses $M$ rungs (target label is placed immediately after the skipped block). Define the enable condition $C \in \{0,1\}$.

**Case 1 — Condition is FALSE (jump is NOT taken):**

The CPU continues sequentially, executing all $N_{total}$ rungs as before. Therefore:

$$
T_{logic,0} = N_{total} \cdot T_0
$$

**Case 2 — Condition is TRUE (jump IS taken):**

The CPU executes the first $K$ rungs, then jumps over $M$ rungs, and resumes at the label, executing the remaining $N_{total} - K - M$ rungs. The skipped $M$ rungs contribute **zero** execution time. Therefore:

$$
T_{logic,1} = (K + N_{total} - K - M) \cdot T_0 = (N_{total} - M) \cdot T_0
$$

**Generalized Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{logic}(C) &= C \cdot T_{logic,1} + (1 - C) \cdot T_{logic,0} \\
&= C \cdot (N_{total} - M) \cdot T_0 + (1 - C) \cdot N_{total} \cdot T_0 \\
&= N_{total} \cdot T_0 - C \cdot M \cdot T_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time saved per scan (when jump is taken):**

$$
\Delta T_{saved} = T_{logic,0} - T_{logic,1} = M \cdot T_0
$$

**Jump effectiveness ratio (instantaneous):**

$$
J_{eff} = \frac{N_{total} - M}{N_{total}} = 1 - \frac{M}{N_{total}}
$$

The longer the skipped block ($M \uparrow$), the greater the scan-time reduction. This is the principal reason engineers use JMP over a long, rarely-needed logic block.

### 3.2 Program Counter Update Logic

The program counter (PC) is the CPU register pointing to the next instruction to execute. Let:

- $PC_{curr}$ = current program counter value
- $LBL_{addr}$ = address of the target label
- $J_{active} \in \{0,1\}$ = jump-active flag (1 = jump taken, 0 = normal)

The update rule is:

$$
PC_{next} = J_{active} \cdot LBL_{addr} + (1 - J_{active}) \cdot (PC_{curr} + 1)
$$

**Verification:**

- If $J_{active} = 1$ (jump taken): $PC_{next} = LBL_{addr}$. ✔ Correct.
- If $J_{active} = 0$ (no jump): $PC_{next} = PC_{curr} + 1$. ✔ Correct (sequential).

### 3.3 MCR vs JMP — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Conditional Jump (JMP / CJP) | Master Control Relay (MCR) |
|---|---|---|
| Rungs inside the zone are **scanned**? | No (skipped entirely) | Yes (executed but outputs overridden) |
| Effect on **non-retentive outputs** | They retain their *last* state | They are **forced to OFF** |
| Effect on **retentive (latched) outputs** | Retained | **Retained** (latched bits are unaffected) |
| Effect on **timers** | Frozen (do not accumulate time) | Reset to zero (in most platforms) |
| Effect on **counters** | Retained (do not increment) | Retained (do not increment) |
| Effect on **scan time** | Reduces scan time | No reduction in scan time |
| Use case | Skip non-relevant logic | Create a hardware-equivalent de-energize zone |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A PLC program has $N_{total} = 200$ rungs. Each rung has an average execution time of $T_0 = 0.05$ ms. A conditional jump at rung 50 skips $M = 80$ rungs. The jump condition is TRUE for 30% of the time ($P(C=1) = 0.3$). Compute the **average logic execution time per scan** with and without the jump.

**Solution:**

Without jump:

$$
T_{logic,nojmp} = 200 \times 0.05 \text{ ms} = 10.0 \text{ ms}
$$

With jump:

$$
T_{logic,jmp} = (10.0 - C \cdot 80 \cdot 0.05) \text{ ms} = (10.0 - 4.0 \cdot C) \text{ ms}
$$

Expected (average) time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T_{logic,jmp}] &= 0.3 \cdot (10.0 - 4.0) + 0.7 \cdot (10.0) \\
&= 0.3 \cdot 6.0 + 0.7 \cdot 10.0 \\
&= 1.8 + 7.0 \\
&= 8.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average time saved per scan:**

$$
\Delta T_{avg} = 10.0 - 8.8 = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{1.2}{10.0} \times 100 = 12\%
$$

This 12% reduction, scaled across millions of scans per day, represents significant CPU bandwidth reclaimed for other tasks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Conditional Jump Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Scan Cycle] --> B[Read All Inputs]
    B --> C[Execute Rung 1]
    C --> D[Execute Rung 2]
    D --> E[Execute Rung K with JMP Instruction]
    E --> F{Jump Condition True?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Load PC with LBL Address]
    G --> H[Skip M Rungs]
    H --> I[Resume Execution at LBL]
    F -- No --> J[Continue to Next Sequential Rung]
    I --> K[Execute Remaining Rungs]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Write All Outputs]
    L --> M[Return to Start of Next Scan]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — JMP vs MCR vs Subroutine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN[Main Program]
        A1[Rung 1 to K-1] --> A2[JMP / CJP Rung]
        A2 --> A3[Post-Label Rungs]
    end
    subgraph SKIP[Skipped Zone]
        B1[Rung K] --> B2[Rung K+1] --> B3[Rung K+M-1]
    end
    A2 -. Condition TRUE .-> SKIP
    A2 -. Condition FALSE .-> A3
    MCR1[MCR Zone] --> MCR2[Forces Non-Retentive Outputs OFF]
    MCR2 --> MCR3[Retentive Outputs Retained]
    SUB1[JSR Call] --> SUB2[Subroutine SBR File]
    SUB2 --> SUB3[RET Return to Main]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Program Execution Flow] --> P1[Sequential Default]
    P1 --> Q1[Use JMP for Skipping]
    P1 --> Q2[Use MCR for Zone De-energize]
    P1 --> Q3[Use JSR for Modular Code]
    Q1 --> R1[Reduces Scan Time]
    Q2 --> R2[No Scan Time Reduction]
    Q3 --> R3[Enables Reusability]
    R1 --> S1[Best for: Mode Selection]
    R2 --> S2[Best for: Safety Zones]
    R3 --> S3[Best for: Recurring Tasks]
```

### 4.4 ASCII Ladder-Logic Representation of a Conditional Jump

```
         I:1/0   I:1/1              JMP     LBL 10
   |-----| |------| |---------------( JMP )---------|
   |   START_A    START_B           ENABLE        |
   |                                              |
   |---[ Skipped Rungs 100 to 199 ]---------------|
   |                                              |
   |  (LBL 10)                                    |
   |---] [                                        |
   |   NEXT_LOGIC_AFTER_JUMP                      |
```

**Reading the diagram:** When both `I:1/0` (START_A) and `I:1/1` (START_B) inputs are TRUE, the JMP coil is energized and the program counter jumps directly to the instruction labeled `LBL 10`, skipping the entire 100-rung block in between.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a Jump Instruction. List the two principal categories of jump instructions used in PLC ladder logic.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Jump Instruction** is a program-control command that alters the normal sequential top-to-bottom execution of the ladder program by transferring the CPU's program counter to a predefined target label, either unconditionally or based on a logical condition.

The **two principal categories** are:
1. **Unconditional Jump (JMP):** Always transfers control to the target label when executed; no logical condition governs the transfer.
2. **Conditional Jump (CJP / JMPC):** Transfer occurs only when the rung's logic continuity is TRUE (i.e., all preceding contacts allow current flow). If FALSE, the jump is ignored and sequential execution continues.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — unconditional; 1 mark — conditional.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between a Jump (JMP) instruction and a Master Control Relay (MCR) instruction in terms of how they affect output devices within their controlled zone.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Jump (JMP) | Master Control Relay (MCR) |
|---|---|---|
| Outputs in the zone | Rungs are **skipped**; outputs retain their **last state** | Rungs **execute**; **non-retentive outputs are forced OFF** |
| Timers | Frozen (no time accumulation) | Reset to zero |
| Scan time | **Reduces** scan time | No reduction in scan time |

*(1 mark per correct point — minimum three distinct differences for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the operational sequence of a **conditional jump** instruction in a PLC ladder program. Illustrate your answer with a labeled ladder diagram showing a jump from `JMP 5` to `LBL 5` that bypasses an interlock-checking sub-routine, and describe what happens during the scan cycle when the enabling input is **(i)** TRUE and **(ii)** FALSE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC program has **250 rungs**, each with an average execution time of **0.04 ms**. A conditional jump placed at **rung 60** skips a block of **90 rungs**. The jump condition remains TRUE for **40%** of the total operating time. Calculate:
  - (i) The logic execution time **per scan** when the jump is taken.
  - (ii) The logic execution time **per scan** when the jump is not taken.
  - (iii) The **average** logic execution time per scan, and the **percentage reduction** in average scan time compared to running without any jump. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer — Question A (a)

**Operational Sequence of a Conditional Jump:**

1. **Initialization:** During each scan cycle, the CPU begins reading inputs and executing rungs sequentially from the top of the program file.

2. **Rung Evaluation:** When the CPU reaches the rung containing the JMP/CJP instruction, the entire contact network to its left is evaluated as a single boolean expression. The result is the **Rung Logic Output (RLO)**.

3. **Decision Point:**
   - **If RLO = TRUE (continuity established):** The jump is *taken*. The CPU's program counter is **loaded with the address of the matching LBL** instruction. All rungs between the JMP and the LBL are **completely bypassed** — they are neither scanned nor executed. Outputs in the bypassed region **retain their previous states** (latched behavior preserved). Timers in the region are **frozen** (do not accumulate time).
   - **If RLO = FALSE (no continuity):** The jump is *ignored*. The JMP instruction behaves as a **NOP (no operation)**. The program counter advances by one, and the next sequential rung is executed as if no JMP existed.

4. **Resumption:** Once the LBL rung is reached (whether by jump or by normal sequential flow), execution continues from that point downward in the usual manner.

**Ladder Diagram:**

```
        START_PB    MODE_AUTO              JMP       LBL 10
   |-----| |---------| |------------------( JMP )------------|
   |                                                (Bypass) |
   |---[ Rung 60 — JMP 5 enabled ]-----------------------|  |
   |                                                         |
   |   (Skipped Rungs 61 to 150 — Interlock Checks)          |
   |   (Not scanned, not executed)                           |
   |                                                         |
   |   (LBL 10)                                              |
   |---] [                                                   |
   |   Main_Conveyor_Start_Rung                              |
   |                                                         |
   |---( )---                                                |
      MOTOR_RUN                                             |
```

**Behavior Summary:**

| Input State | Jump Active? | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `START_PB = 1`, `MODE_AUTO = 1` | YES | PC → LBL 10; Rungs 61–150 skipped |
| `START_PB = 0` or `MODE_AUTO = 0` | NO | Rungs 61–150 executed normally |

*(Marks distribution: 1 — definition; 2 — operational steps; 2 — ladder diagram; 2 — TRUE/FALSE behavior.)*

##### Model Answer — Question A (b)

**Given:**
- $N_{total} = 250$ rungs
- $T_0 = 0.04$ ms per rung
- Jump at rung $K = 60$
- Skipped block $M = 90$ rungs
- $P(C = 1) = 0.40$ (jump condition TRUE for 40% of the time)

**Step 1 — Logic execution time when jump is taken ($C = 1$):**

The executed rungs are: rungs 1 through 60 (i.e., the JMP rung itself and all above), plus rungs 151 through 250 (after the label). Total executed = $60 + (250 - 60 - 90) = 60 + 100 = 160$ rungs.

$$
T_{logic,1} = 160 \times 0.04 \text{ ms} = 6.40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Logic execution time when jump is NOT taken ($C = 0$):**

All 250 rungs execute sequentially.

$$
T_{logic,0} = 250 \times 0.04 \text{ ms} = 10.00 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Average logic execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T_{logic}] &= 0.40 \times 6.40 + 0.60 \times 10.00 \\
&= 2.56 + 6.00 \\
&= 8.56 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction in average scan time:**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{10.00 - 8.56}{10.00} \times 100 = \frac{1.44}{10.00} \times 100 = 14.4\%
$$

**Final Results:**
- (i) $T_{logic,1} = 6.40$ ms
- (ii) $T_{logic,0} = 10.00$ ms
- (iii) $E[T_{logic}] = 8.56$ ms with a **14.4% reduction** in average scan time.

*(Marks distribution: 1 — identifying executed rungs; 2 — parts (i) and (ii); 2 — expected value; 2 — percentage reduction.)*

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a)** With a clear block diagram and truth table, explain the operation of a **Master Control Relay (MCR)** instruction. Explain how it differs from a simple JMP in its treatment of **(i)** non-retentive outputs, **(ii)** retentive (latched) outputs, **(iii)** timers, and **(iv)** scan time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a ladder logic program for the following application: A conveyor system has **two modes — AUTO and MANUAL**, selected by a selector switch `I:1/0`. In **AUTO** mode, the conveyor starts 5 seconds after `START_PB` (`I:1/1`) is pressed and runs until `STOP_PB` (`I:1/2`) is pressed. In **MANUAL** mode, the conveyor runs only while `JOG_PB` (`I:1/3`) is held. Use a **conditional jump** to bypass the AUTO logic when MANUAL is selected, and a **second conditional jump** to bypass the MANUAL logic when AUTO is selected. Draw the complete ladder program with at least two skipped zones and explain the scan-cycle behavior. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer — Question B (a)

**Master Control Relay (MCR) — Operation:**

An MCR instruction works in pairs: an **MCR start** rung and an **MCR end** rung. It defines a *zone* in the program. While the controlling rung of the MCR is FALSE, **all non-retentive outputs within the zone are forced to OFF**, regardless of their rung logic.

**Block Diagram:**

```
  MCR_EN  --------[ MCR START ]-------- MCR ZONE (Force non-retentive OUTs OFF)
                                          |
                                          |--- ( ) Non-Retentive Output 1  →  FORCED OFF
                                          |
                                          |--- TON Timer 1               →  RESET to 0
                                          |
                                          |--- ( L ) Latched Output 1    →  RETAINED
                                          |
                                          |--- CTU Counter 1             →  RETAINED
                                          |
                                       [ MCR END ]
```

**Truth Table for Outputs inside MCR Zone:**

| MCR_Enable | Non-Retentive Output State | Retentive (Latched) Output State | Timer State | Counter State |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| TRUE (1) | Follows rung logic | Follows rung logic | Accumulates | Increments |
| FALSE (0) | **Forced OFF** | **Retained (last value)** | **Reset to 0** | **Retained** |

**Differences from a Simple JMP:**

| Aspect | JMP | MCR |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Non-retentive outputs | Retain last state | **Forced OFF** |
| (ii) Retentive outputs | Retained | **Retained** |
| (iii) Timers | Frozen (no accumulation) | **Reset to 0** |
| (iv) Scan time | **Reduced** (rungs skipped) | **Unchanged** (rungs executed) |

*(Marks distribution: 2 — block diagram; 2 — truth table; 3 — four-way comparison.)*

##### Model Answer — Question B (b)

**Application Design — Two-Mode Conveyor Control Using Conditional Jumps**

**Ladder Program:**

```
        I:1/0  (AUTO/MANUAL Selector = 0 for AUTO)        JMP       LBL 20
   |----| / |------------------------------------------------( JMP )-------|
   |   NOT_AUTO  (Jump to MANUAL block if NOT in AUTO)                      |
   |                                                                       |
   |   (LBL 10)                                                           |
   |   START_PB   STOP_PB                                                 |
   |---| |--------|/|--------[TON  T4:1, PRE=50]--------------------------|
   |   AUTO_START         5-sec delay                                    |
   |                                                                       |
   |   T4:1/DN  ───( )─── CONVEYOR_RUN                                    |
   |   (Auto run after delay)                                             |
   |                                                                       |
   |   (LBL 20)                                                           |
   |   AUTO/MANUAL Selector = 1 for MANUAL        JMP       LBL 30        |
   |---| |------------------------------------------------( JMP )-------|
   |   IS_MANUAL  (Jump to END if in MANUAL — bypass AUTO zone)         |
   |                                                                       |
   |   (LBL 30)                                                           |
   |   JOG_PB                                                          |
   |---| |------------------------------------------------------------( )---|
   |   MANUAL_JOG                                       CONVEYOR_RUN        |
   |                                                                       |
   |   END                                                                |
```

**Explanation of Scan-Cycle Behavior:**

1. **In AUTO mode** (`I:1/0 = 0`): `NOT_AUTO` is TRUE → first JMP to `LBL 20` is taken → the JOG logic at LBL 30 is skipped → only the AUTO sequence executes. The conveyor starts 5 seconds after `START_PB` and runs continuously.

2. **In MANUAL mode** (`I:1/0 = 1`): `NOT_AUTO` is FALSE → first JMP ignored → AUTO logic executes (but its `START_PB` is not pressed in manual operation, so harmless) → reaches the second JMP at LBL 20 → `IS_MANUAL` is TRUE → JMP to `LBL 30` → JOG logic runs. Conveyor runs only while `JOG_PB` is held.

3. **Mode isolation:** The two JMPs ensure that **only the logic corresponding to the active mode is executed**, preventing unintended output contention and reducing scan time.

**Advantages realized:**
- Mode-specific logic is **isolated** in clearly labeled sections.
- **Scan time is reduced** by approximately 50% because only one mode block executes per scan.
- **Maintenance is easier** — modifying AUTO logic does not affect MANUAL logic.

*(Marks distribution: 3 — ladder diagram; 2 — JMP placement; 2 — scan-cycle explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing MCR with JMP:** Students frequently claim MCR skips rungs like JMP. **MCR executes the rungs but overrides outputs; JMP skips the rungs entirely.** This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting retentive output behavior:** Under MCR, latched outputs are *retained*, not forced OFF. Many textbooks oversimplify and lose a mark.
> 3. **Wrong jump address:** LBL number must match the JMP number exactly. A mismatch causes a PLC fault.
> 4. **Ignoring scan-time implications:** Always state whether the instruction *reduces* scan time (JMP yes, MCR no).
> 5. **Nested jumps:** Some platforms restrict or forbid nested JMPs. Mention this only if explicitly asked.
> 6. **Unit errors in numericals:** $T_0$ is in **ms**, not seconds. $E[T_{logic}]$ should carry consistent units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Jump Instruction (JMP):** Transfers program control to a matching LBL; can be **unconditional** or **conditional** (rung-true).
- [x] **Master Control Relay (MCR):** Forces **non-retentive outputs OFF** within a zone; does **not** skip rungs; does **not** reduce scan time.
- [x] **Subroutine (JSR/SBR/RET):** Modular, reusable code; control returns to the calling point.
- [x] **Scan time formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{logic} + T_{out} + T_{overhead}$.
- [x] **Logic time with jump:** $T_{logic}(C) = N_{total} \cdot T_0 - C \cdot M \cdot T_0$.
- [x] **Program counter update:** $PC_{next} = J_{active} \cdot LBL_{addr} + (1 - J_{active}) \cdot (PC_{curr}+1)$.
- [x] **Jump effectiveness:** $J_{eff} = 1 - M / N_{total}$.
- [x] **LBL–JMP pairing is mandatory**; numbers must match exactly.
- [x] **Timers in JMP zones are frozen**; **timers in MCR zones are reset**.
- [x] **Counters are always retained** under both JMP and MCR.
- [x] **Manufacturer variants:** Allen-Bradley `JMP/LBL`, Siemens `JC/JCN`, Mitsubishi `CJ/SCJ`, Omron `JMP(04)/CJP(05)`.
- [x] **Standard scan time target:** **10–20 ms per 1K instructions** in most modular PLCs.
- [x] **Use MCR for safety zones (E-stop); use JMP for mode selection; use JSR for recurring sub-tasks.**
- [x] **Common KTU 14-mark question pattern:** Theory explanation (7) + numerical scan-time problem (7), or ladder-program design (7) + comparison table (7).
- [x] **Board tip:** Always draw a labeled ladder diagram for any "explain with example" question — diagrams carry 30–40% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Handling Instructions** in a PLC are a class of ladder logic instructions used to **move, compare, convert, manipulate, and process binary, BCD, and integer numerical data** within the processor's memory (data files) and I/O image tables. They operate on **16-bit (single word)** or **32-bit (double/long word)** data and do **not** change the logic continuity (rung condition) on their own, except for comparison-type instructions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Data Handling Instructions form a sub-group of the **Output/Programming Instructions** that fall under the broader category of **Bit, Word, and File Instructions** in Allen-Bradley (AB) PLC-5 / SLC 500 family, which is the standard reference architecture taught in this module.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Filing Clerk 🗂️**
> Think of a PLC processor memory as a giant office with thousands of locked filing cabinets (each cabinet = a 16-bit word like `N7:0`, `T4:0`, `C5:0`). 
> - **Move (MOV)** = A clerk who opens one cabinet, reads the paper inside, and copies it into another cabinet. The original is unchanged.
> - **Compare (EQU, GRT)** = A clerk who reads two cabinets and raises a hand (energises the rung) if one paper says "500" and the other says "300" and the instruction is "Greater Than".
> - **Math (ADD, MUL)** = A clerk with a calculator who adds papers from two cabinets and stores the result in a third.
> - **Masked Move (MVM)** = A clerk with a stencil that hides certain digits, so only some parts are copied.
> - **Convert (TOD, FRD)** = A translator who converts a number from "pencil and paper" (decimal) to "barcode" (BCD) or vice-versa.

## 1.3 Classification of Data Handling Instructions

For KTU Board examinations, the following classification is **mandatory** to memorise:

| Sub-Family | Common Instructions (AB PLC-5 / SLC 500) | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Transfer / Move** | `MOV`, `MVM` | Copy a word value to another word |
| **Data Comparison** | `EQU`, `NEQ`, `GRT`, `GEQ`, `LES`, `LEQ`, `LIM` | Compare two values, output a boolean |
| **Data Arithmetic** | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `SQR`, `CLR` | Mathematical operations on 16-bit words |
| **Data Logical / Bitwise** | `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT` | Bit-by-bit Boolean logic on words |
| **Data Conversion** | `TOD` (BCD→Binary), `FRD` (Binary→BCD), `DCD`, `ENC`, `DEG`, `RAD` | Change data format/representation |
| **Data Shift / Rotate** | `BSL`, `BSR` | Shift bits through a word/file |
| **Data File** | `COP`, `FLL`, `BTD` | Operate on entire files/blocks of words |
| **FIFO / LIFO** | `FFL`, `FFU`, `LFL`, `LFU` | Queue/Stack operations for serial data |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard metric** for data size in AB PLCs is the **16-bit WORD (range 32,768 to +32,767)**. Operations exceeding this **overflow** the word and set processor status flags (e.g., **S:0/0 = Carry, S:0/1 = Overflow**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Instruction Family

### 2.1.1 MOVE Instructions

- **MOV (Move)**: When the rung is TRUE, the content of the *Source* address is copied into the *Destination* address. The source value is **not** altered.
- **MVM (Masked Move)**: A 16-bit **Mask** word is applied. For every bit set to `1` in the mask, the corresponding source bit is passed to the destination. For every bit set to `0` in the mask, the destination bit is left untouched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MVM?** Used in industrial applications to extract a specific group of bits from a 16-bit status word (e.g., isolate the high 8 bits which represent an error code, while leaving the low 8 bits, which are process flags, untouched).

### 2.1.2 Comparison Instructions

These are the **only** data handling instructions that **affect the rung's logic continuity** — they output a TRUE/FALSE boolean.

- **EQU (Equal)**, **NEQ (Not Equal)**, **GRT (Greater Than)**, **GEQ (Greater Than or Equal)**, **LES (Less Than)**, **LEQ (Less Than or Equal)**: Compare Source A with Source B.
- **LIM (Limit Test)**: TRUE if the test value lies within a specified upper and lower limit (used for alarm window checking).

### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Instructions

- **ADD, SUB**: Operate on 16-bit signed integers.
- **MUL, DIV**: 16-bit operations that **automatically use 32-bit accumulator registers** in PLC-5 to prevent overflow (`Math Register = S:13` and `S:14`).
- **CLR (Clear)**: Resets a word to **0**.
- **SQR (Square Root)**: Computes $\sqrt{x}$.

### 2.1.4 Logical (Bitwise) Instructions

- **AND, OR, XOR**: Perform bit-by-bit logical operations on the 16-bit words.
- **NOT**: Inverts all 16 bits (1's complement).

### 2.1.5 Conversion Instructions

- **TOD (To Decimal / BCD)**: Reads a **BCD** value from a source and stores its **Binary** equivalent in the destination.
- **FRD (From Decimal / BCD)**: Reads a **Binary** value and stores the **BCD** equivalent (used to drive 7-segment displays).
- **DCD (Decode)**: A 1-of-16 decoder — sets the bit position (0–15) in the destination corresponding to the value in the source.
- **ENC (Encode)**: Finds the highest set bit position and stores it as a number in the destination (reverse of DCD).

## 2.2 KTU Formula & Range Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for any numerical problem in the KTU University Exam on Data Handling Instructions.

| Operation | Formula / Mechanism | Boundary / Range | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16-bit Word (Integer) | $-2^{15} \le V \le 2^{15}-1$ | **−32,768 to +32,767** | Signed, 2's complement |
| BCD Digit Range | $0 \le d \le 9$ per nibble | Each hex digit must be 0–9 | Invalid BCD = Processor Fault |
| MOV Operation | $D_{new} = S$ | No change to S | Bit-level copy |
| MVM Operation | $D_{new} = (D_{old} \land \overline{M}) \lor (S \land M)$ | $M$ = 16-bit mask | Mask `1` = transfer; `0` = hold |
| ADD / SUB | $R = A \pm B$ | Detect: $S:0/0$ (Carry), $S:0/1$ (Overflow) | 16-bit result |
| MUL (16-bit) | $R = A \times B$ | 32-bit result in $S:13$ (low) & $S:14$ (high) | $S:13 = \text{low word}$, $S:14 = \text{high word}$ |
| DIV (16-bit) | $R = A / B$ | Quotient in $S:14$, Remainder in $S:13$ | Divide-by-zero → fault |
| SQR | $R = \lfloor \sqrt{A} \rfloor$ | Integer truncated | $A \ge 0$ |
| AND | $D = A \ \& \ B$ bitwise | Logical AND per bit | Boolean mask |
| OR | $D = A \ \vert \ B$ bitwise | Logical OR per bit | Setting bits |
| XOR | $D = A \oplus B$ bitwise | Logical XOR per bit | Toggling bits |
| NOT | $D = \overline{A}$ bitwise | 1's complement | 16-bit inversion |
| TOD | $D = \text{DEC}(S_{BCD})$ | $S$ must be valid BCD (each nibble 0–9) | BCD → Binary |
| FRD | $D = \text{BCD}(S_{BIN})$ | Result valid for 7-segment | Binary → BCD |
| DCD | Bit $n$ in $D = 1$, if $S = n$ | $0 \le S \le 15$ | 1-of-16 |
| ENC | $D = \text{position of highest 1-bit in S}$ | $0 \le D \le 15$ | 16-to-4 priority |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Note:**
> - **MOV** is used in **recipe management** — copying a stored parameter set (e.g., 50 production values) from a preset data file (`N12`) to the active process file (`N7`) when a "Run" button is pressed.
> - **MVM** is critical in **fault diagnostics** — masking out irrelevant bits of a 16-bit fault word to display only the critical alarm on an HMI.
> - **TOD/FRD** is mandatory when interfacing PLCs with **7-segment LED displays, thumbwheel switches, and BCD-encoded encoders**.
> - **DCD/ENC** are widely used in **barcode scanners, traffic-light multiplexing, and stepper motor commutation logic** where 1-of-N selection is required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — MVM (Masked Move)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A PLC receives a 16-bit status word `N7:10 = 0xA53F` (Hex). The system only needs the **lower 8 bits** (low byte) for an HMI display. The upper 8 bits must remain unchanged. Compute the output of an `MVM` instruction with:
> - Source = `N7:10`
> - Mask = `0x00FF`
> - Destination = `N7:11`

### Step-by-Step Solution

The MVM operation in Allen-Bradley follows the formal equation:

$$D_{new} = (D_{old} \ \land \ \overline{M}) \ \lor \ (S \ \land \ M)$$

**Step 1 — Convert given values to binary for visual clarity.**

$$S = N7{:}10 = 0xA53F = 1010\ 0101\ 0011\ 1111_2$$

$$M = 0x00FF = 0000\ 0000\ 1111\ 1111_2$$

$$D_{old} = N7{:}11 = 0x0000 \ (\text{assume initialised})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverted mask $\overline{M}$.**

$$\overline{M} = 1111\ 1111\ 0000\ 0000_2 = 0xFF00$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(D_{old} \ \land \ \overline{M})$ — i.e., destination bits protected by mask.**

Since $D_{old} = 0x0000$:

$$D_{old} \ \land \ \overline{M} = 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2 = 0x0000$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(S \ \land \ M)$ — source bits allowed to pass through mask.**

$$S \ \land \ M = 1010\ 0101\ 0011\ 1111_2 \ \land \ 0000\ 0000\ 1111\ 1111_2 = 0000\ 0000\ 0011\ 1111_2 = 0x003F$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final OR of Step 3 and Step 4.**

$$D_{new} = 0x0000 \ \lor \ 0x003F = 0x003F = 0011\ 1111_2$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

Only the **lower 8 bits of N7:10 (`0011 1111` = `0x3F` = decimal 63)** were copied into `N7:11`. The upper 8 bits of the destination remain `0` (protected by the mask). This is the classical **"extract low-byte"** pattern used in serial communication decoding.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Arithmetic Division with Status Flags

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In an SLC-500, perform `DIV 100 / 7` and store the result. Identify which status bits get set if the input rung is TRUE.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the DIV register convention.**

In AB PLC-5 / SLC 500, after a 16-bit division, the result is stored across two words:

- **Math Register Low Word = `S:13`** → contains the **Remainder**.
- **Math Register High Word = `S:14`** → contains the **Quotient**.

**Step 2 — Perform integer division.**

$$100 \div 7 = 14 \text{ remainder } 2$$

**Step 3 — Populate the math registers.**

$$S:14 = 14 \ (\text{Quotient})$$

$$S:13 = 2 \ (\text{Remainder})$$

**Step 4 — Examine processor status flags.**

- **`S:0/0` (Carry)**: Reset (0), because the operation completed within 16-bit range for the quotient ($14 < 32,768$).
- **`S:0/1` (Overflow)**: Reset (0), because no signed-overflow occurred.
- **`S:0/2` (Zero)**: Reset (0), because the result is non-zero.
- **`S:0/3` (Sign)**: Reset (0), because the result is positive.

**Step 5 — Engineer interpretation.**

The technician can read the remainder `S:13` to determine leftover material after a cut-to-length operation, while the quotient `S:14` gives the integer number of complete cuts. This is **canonical KTU-style industrial arithmetic**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 — TOD and FRD Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A thumbwheel switch inputs a BCD value of `0x0297` (decimal 297 equivalent) into `I:001`. The PLC must convert it to a binary integer and then output it back as BCD to a 7-segment display at `O:003`. Show both ladder steps and the math register evolution.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Input value (BCD format).**

$$S_{BCD} = 0x0297$$

**Step 2 — Validate the BCD digits.**

$$\text{Nibbles: } 0, 2, 9, 7 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{All in range } 0 \text{ to } 9 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Valid BCD}$$

**Step 3 — Apply `TOD` (BCD → Binary).**

$$D = \text{DEC}(S_{BCD}) = 297$$

Mathematically:

$$D = (0 \times 4096) + (2 \times 256) + (9 \times 16) + (7 \times 1) = 512 + 144 + 7 = 663 \text{ (wait, recalculate)}$$

Recomputing (standard positional weight for 4-digit BCD = thousands, hundreds, tens, units):

$$D = (0 \times 1000) + (2 \times 100) + (9 \times 10) + (7 \times 1) = 0 + 200 + 90 + 7 = 297$$

**Step 4 — Store intermediate binary value in `N7:5`.**

$$N7:5 = 297$$

**Step 5 — Apply `FRD` (Binary → BCD).**
