> This is a typical 7-mark KTU numerical.

### Problem
A temperature control loop has:
- $SP = 75 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $PV_n = 70 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $PV_{n-1} = 68 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
- $K_c = 2.0$, $T_i = 5\,\mathrm{min}$, $T_d = 0.5\,\mathrm{min}$, $T_s = 1\,\mathrm{s}$

Compute $CV_n$ using the **discrete position form**.

### Step 1 — Compute Current Error
$$
e_n \;=\; SP - PV_n \;=\; 75 - 70 \;=\; 5 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Previous Error
$$
e_{n-1} \;=\; SP - PV_{n-1} \;=\; 75 - 68 \;=\; 7 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Proportional Term
$$
CV_P \;=\; K_c \cdot e_n \;=\; 2.0 \times 5 \;=\; 10.0
$$

### Step 4 — Derivative Term
$$
CV_D \;=\; \dfrac{K_c \cdot T_d}{T_s}\,(e_n - e_{n-1}) \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 \times 0.5 \times 60}{1}\,(5 - 7)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit conversion trap:** $T_d$ is given in **minutes** but $T_s$ in **seconds**. Convert: $T_d = 0.5 \times 60 = 30\,\mathrm{s}$.

$$
CV_D \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 \times 30}{1}\,(-2) \;=\; -120.0
$$

### Step 5 — Integral Term (assume $\sum e_k = 40$ from prior scans)
$$
CV_I \;=\; \dfrac{K_c \cdot T_s}{T_i}\sum e_k \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 \times 1}{300}\,(40) \;=\; 0.2667
$$

### Step 6 — Final Output
$$
CV_n \;=\; CV_P + CV_I + CV_D \;=\; 10.0 + 0.2667 - 120.0 \;=\; -109.7333
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Anti-Windup Clamp
If output limits are $0 \leq CV \leq 100$:

$$
CV_n^{\text{final}} \;=\; 0 \quad (\text{saturated at lower bound})
$$

### Step 8 — Interpretation
The PV is rising toward SP ($e_n < e_{n-1}$), so the derivative action is **negative** (decelerate heating). Despite a positive $K_c e_n$, the controller is already fully off, correctly anticipating that the temperature will hit the target.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner pitfall:** Forgetting to convert $T_d$ (min) and $T_i$ (min) into seconds when $T_s$ is in seconds is the **#1 mistake** in KTU PID numericals.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Instruction Categories

| Instruction Class | Example | Data Type | Typical Use in PID Loop | Side-Effect on Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Math | ADD, MUL | Word / Integer / Float | Feedforward summing into CV | None |
| Comparison | GRT, LIM | Word / Integer | Alarm, mode switching | Triggers Boolean rung |
| Move | MOV, MVM | Word | Loads SP, $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ into control block | Overwrites destination |
| Scaling | SCL, SCP | Integer → Integer | Maps ADC raw to engineering units | Round-off / truncation |
| Logical | AND, OR | Word (bitwise) | Status-word decoding | None |
| File / Shift | BSL, BSR | Bit file | Tracking-shift register for diagnostics | Updates file in place |
| Sequencer | SQO, SQC | Word file | Profile-driven SP ramp | Steps pointer on transition |
| Program Flow | JMP, SBR | n/a | Scan-time segmentation | Alters scan execution path |
| Advanced | PID | Integer / Float | Closed-loop regulation | Writes CV, updates integrator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop PID Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spSrc[Setpoint Source SP] -->|EU| errorCalc
    pvSrc[Process Variable PV from Sensor] -->|EU| errorCalc
    spSrc --> kcBlock
    pvSrc --> kcBlock
    errorCalc[Error Node e equals SP minus PV] --> sumNode
    kcBlock[Proportional Gain Kc multiplier] --> sumNode
    tiBlock[Integral Term Ki over Ti integrator with anti windup clamp] --> sumNode
    tdBlock[Derivative Term Kc times Td differentiator] --> sumNode
    sumNode[PID Sum Node adds P plus I plus D] --> cvOut[Control Variable CV output]
    cvOut --> actuator[Final Control Element Valve Heater VFD]
    actuator --> plant[Plant Process]
    plant -->|EU| pvSrc
    modeSel[Mode Selector Auto Manual Cascade] --> kcBlock
    modeSel --> tiBlock
    modeSel --> tdBlock
    cvOut --> clampNode[Clamp CV between CVmin and CVmax]
    clampNode --> cvOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the **three parallel branches** (P, I, D) summing into the output, with **negative feedback** from the plant back to PV. Anti-windup is enforced at the CV-clamp node.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — PID Algorithm Execution Flow (Per Scan)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Scan Starts]) --> readPV[Read PV from analog input]
    readPV --> readSP[Read SP from HMI or program]
    readSP --> calcErr[Compute e equals SP minus PV]
    calcErr --> pTerm[P term Kc times e]
    calcErr --> iTerm[I term Ki times sum of e over time]
    calcErr --> dTerm[D term Kd times de over dt backward difference]
    pTerm --> sum[Sum P plus I plus D]
    iTerm --> sum
    dTerm --> sum
    sum --> clamp{CV within CVmin and CVmax}
    clamp -->|Yes| out[Write CV to analog output]
    clamp -->|No| limit[Clamp CV and freeze integrator]
    limit --> out
    out --> wait[Wait for loop update time Ts]
    wait --> start
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — "Other Important Instructions" Categorical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Other Important PLC Instructions]
    root --> m1[Math Instructions]
    root --> c1[Comparison Instructions]
    root --> mv1[Move and Logical Instructions]
    root --> f1[File and Shift Instructions]
    root --> s1[Sequencer Instructions]
    root --> p1[Program Flow Control]
    root --> a1[Advanced and Process Instructions]

    m1 --> m1a[ADD SUB MUL DIV]
    m1 --> m1b[SCL SCP ABS SQR]

    c1 --> c1a[EQU NEQ GRT GEQ LES LEQ]
    c1 --> c1b[LIM MEQ]

    mv1 --> mv1a[MOV MVM]
    mv1 --> mv1b[AND OR XOR NOT BTD]

    f1 --> f1a[BSL BSR FFL FFU COP FLL]

    s1 --> s1a[SQO SQL SQC]

    p1 --> p1a[JMP LBL SBR RET]
    p1 --> p1b[MCR TND SUS]

    a1 --> a1a[PID]
    a1 --> a1b[MSG IIM IOM SVC]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PID With Surrounding Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hmi[HMI Panel Operator Entry SP] --> mov1[MOV instruction writes SP to control block]
    sensor[Field Sensor 4 to 20 mA or RTD] --> scl1[SCL SCP converts raw ADC to engineering units]
    scl1 --> pvTag[PV engineering units tag]
    mov1 --> pidBlock[PID instruction block control file]
    pvTag --> pidBlock
    pidBlock --> cvTag[CV control variable]
    cvTag --> scl2[SCL SCP converts CV back to raw output counts]
    scl2 --> aout[Analog output module to valve or VFD]
    cvTag --> limchk[LIM comparison for alarm high and low CV]
    cvTag --> addFF[ADD MUL for feedforward compensation]
    addFF --> pidBlock
    pidBlock --> mode[JMP SBR MCR scan segmentation for multi loop]
    cvTag --> sqo[Sequencer SQO batch recipe profile drives SP]
    sqo --> mov1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The examiner often asks *"List and explain the instructions surrounding a PID block in a typical closed-loop project."* The above architecture is the canonical answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Define the PID instruction in a PLC. State the role of each of the three terms — Proportional, Integral, and Derivative — in a closed-loop control system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The PID instruction is a **closed-loop, function-block instruction** that computes a controller output **CV** as a function of the error $e = SP - PV$.
- **(1 mark)** **Proportional** term $K_c \cdot e$ gives an immediate, linear correction; larger $K_c$ reduces rise time but increases overshoot.
- **(1 mark)** **Integral** term $(K_c / T_i) \int e \, dt$ eliminates steady-state offset; **Derivative** term $K_c T_d (de/dt)$ anticipates future error and damps oscillations.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*List any four "Other Important Instructions" used in PLC programming and state one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — pick any four, 0.75 each):**

| Instruction | Application |
|---|---|
| **SCL** | Converting raw 0–32767 ADC input to °C engineering units |
| **MOV** | Loading PID tuning constants from HMI into the control block |
| **GRT** | High-temperature alarm trigger above threshold |
| **SQO** | Sequencer for batch-process recipe profile (e.g., ramp/soak) |
| **JMP** | Skipping non-critical scan segments during fault conditions |

---

## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Derive the discrete-time (position-form) PID equation from the continuous-time ideal PID equation. Clearly state the role of the loop update time $T_s$ and the conversion of the integral and derivative terms.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Continuous-time ideal PID (1 mark):**
$$
CV(t) \;=\; K_c e(t) \;+\; \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\!\int_0^{t}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_c T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**(ii) Sampling at $t = nT_s$ (1 mark):**
$$
CV_n \;=\; K_c e_n \;+\; \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\!\int_0^{nT_s}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_c T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}\Big|_{nT_s}
$$

**(iii) Rectangular backward-Euler integration (2 marks):**
$$
\!\int_0^{nT_s}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k
$$

**(iv) Backward-difference derivative (2 marks):**
$$
\dfrac{de}{dt}\Big|_{nT_s} \;\approx\; \dfrac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}
$$

**(v) Final assembled equation (1 mark):**
$$
CV_n \;=\; K_c e_n \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\!\sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}\,(e_n - e_{n-1})
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the phenomenon of **integral windup** in a PID controller. Describe any **two techniques** used by PLC PID blocks to prevent it.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition (2 marks):** Integral windup occurs when a **sustained error** (e.g., actuator already saturated at 100 % open but PV still below SP) causes the integral accumulator $\sum e_k$ to grow unbounded. When the error finally reverses, the integrator must "unwind" — producing a large overshoot.

**(ii) Technique 1 — Output Clamping (2 marks):** The PLC enforces $CV_{\min} \leq CV \leq CV_{\max}$. When the computed (unsaturated) CV exceeds the limit, the actual CV is held at the boundary.

**(iii) Technique 2 — Conditional Integration (Anti-windup freeze) (2 marks):** Whenever CV is in saturation, the integrator is **disabled** — new errors are not accumulated until the output re-enters its linear range.

**(iv) Optional bonus technique — Back-calculation (1 mark):** A feedback signal $(CV_{\text{out}} - CV_{\text{unsaturated}}) / T_t$ is added to the integrator input, where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **closed-loop PID control system** as implemented in a PLC. Label all signals and identify the role of each block.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Block diagram description (3 marks):** A closed-loop PID system consists of:
- **Setpoint input SP** (desired value, e.g., 75 °C)
- **Subtractor** computing $e = SP - PV$
- **PID block** computing $CV = K_c e + (K_c/T_i)\int e\,dt + K_c T_d (de/dt)$
- **Final Control Element** (valve, VFD, heater)
- **Plant/Process**
- **Sensor/transmitter** returning $PV$
- **Anti-windup clamp** on $CV$

**(ii) Signal flow explanation (2 marks):** The PID block resides inside the PLC. The PV is read from an analog input module (e.g., 0–10 V from a thermocouple transmitter). The SP is written via HMI or program logic. CV is output as 4–20 mA to the actuator.

**(iii) Role of each block (2 marks):**
- **Subtractor:** Computes instantaneous error.
- **PID block:** Continuous correction.
- **Actuator:** Manipulates process energy/material.
- **Plant:** The physical system (tank, furnace, motor).
- **Sensor:** Closes the loop; without it, control would be open-loop.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compare **position form** and **velocity (incremental) form** of the discrete PID equation. State **two advantages** of the velocity form.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Position form (2 marks):**
$$
CV_n \;=\; K_c e_n \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\!\sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}\,(e_n - e_{n-1})
$$
Computes the absolute value of CV; requires memory of entire integral sum.

**(ii) Velocity form (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta CV_n \;=\; K_c(e_n - e_{n-1}) \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\,e_n \;+\; \dfrac{K_c T_d}{T_s}\,(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})
$$
Computes only the change in CV; no growing integral.

**(iii) Advantages of velocity form (3 marks):**
1. **No integral windup** — since no integral accumulator exists, saturation is impossible.
2. **Bumpless transfer** — switching from Manual → Auto does not cause a CV jump, because CV is reconstructed by summation: $CV_n = CV_{n-1} + \Delta CV_n$.
3. *(Bonus)* Naturally suited to **stepper motors and incremental valves** where the output is a pulse count.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Module 2 PID questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — $T_i$ and $T_d$ given in *minutes*, $T_s$ in *seconds*. Always convert $T_i$, $T_d$ to seconds before substituting. *(–1 to –2 marks)*
> 2. **Wrong sign of derivative term** — derivative is $(e_n - e_{n-1})$, *not* $(e_{n-1} - e_n)$. Confusing the sign leads to oscillation instead of damping. *(–2 marks)*
> 3. **Forgetting anti-windup** — when CV is clamped at 100 % or 0 %, simply ignoring the saturation (instead of freezing the integrator) is a serious conceptual error. *(–1 mark)*
> 4. **Confusing $K_p$ (process gain) with $K_c$ (controller gain)** — these are *different* quantities. $K_p$ comes from the open-loop step test; $K_c$ is the controller tuning parameter. *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Mixing up PID with other instructions** — students often write MOV / ADD logic when the question asks specifically about PID control block structure. Read the question stem carefully!

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PID** stands for **Proportional–Integral–Derivative** — a **closed-loop** control algorithm.
- **Continuous form:** $CV = K_c e + (K_c/T_i)\int e\,dt + K_c T_d (de/dt)$
- **Discrete position form:** $CV_n = K_c e_n + (K_c T_s/T_i)\sum e_k + (K_c T_d/T_s)(e_n - e_{n-1})$
- **Velocity form** computes $\Delta CV_n$ — immune to windup; ideal for stepper outputs.
- **Error:** $e = SP - PV$
- **Three term roles:** P → immediate correction; I → eliminates offset; D → damps & anticipates.
- **Loop update time $T_s$** must be ≥ 10× faster than the process time constant.
- **Anti-windup** is mandatory in real PLC PID blocks — clamp CV, freeze integrator, or back-calculate.
- **Tuning methods:** Ziegler–Nichols (ultimate-gain & reaction-curve), Cohen–Coon, Lambda.
- **Other Important Instructions** fall into 8 families: Math, Comparison, Move/Logical, File/Shift, Sequencer, Program Flow, Advanced/Process.
- **SCL / SCP** are the workhorses for analog scaling around PID.
- **MOV** loads tuning parameters from HMI into the PID control block.
- **GRT / LIM** generate high/low alarms around the PID output.
- **SQO** generates SP profiles for batch / ramp-soak processes.
- **JMP / SBR** segment scan time across multiple PID loops.
- **Always remember to clamp $CV$ between $CV_{\min}$ and $CV_{\max}$.**
- **Convert all time units to a common base (seconds) before any computation.**
- **Derivative on PV** avoids "derivative kick" from setpoint changes.
- **Position form** suits analog outputs; **velocity form** suits incremental outputs.
- **PID block is stateful** — its output depends on prior samples, unlike stateless math instructions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers and Counters in PLC: Problems, Design, Development, and Simulation of PLC Programs for Temperature Control and Valve Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timers and Counters in PLC: Problems, Design, Development, and Simulation of PLC Programs for Temperature Control and Valve Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Timers in PLC — Formal Academic Definition

A **Timer** in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a dedicated internal instruction (soft-element) that measures elapsed time against a user-defined **Preset Value (PV)** and generates a Boolean output (TRUE/FALSE) based on the relationship between the **Accumulated Value (AC)** and the preset. Timers are the temporal nerve-centers of sequential automation, enabling time-delayed switching, dwell-time enforcement, and pulse generation.

**KTU 2024 Standard Classification of PLC Timers:**

| Timer Type | IEC 61131-3 Symbol | Functional Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| On-Delay Timer | **TON** | Output turns ON after a fixed delay once input goes TRUE |
| Off-Delay Timer | **TOF** | Output remains ON for a fixed delay after input goes FALSE |
| Pulse Timer | **TP** | Output generates a fixed-width pulse on input edge |
| Retentive On-Delay | **RTO / TONR** | Accumulates time across power cycles; requires explicit reset |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The **Time Base (Resolution)** is the heartbeat of a PLC timer. Standard bases are **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, and 1 s**. The actual time elapsed is given by:
> $$T_{elapsed} = \text{PV} \times \text{Time Base}$$
> Example: PV = 50 with TB = 100 ms → T = 50 × 0.1 s = **5 seconds**.

---

## 1.2 Counters in PLC — Formal Academic Definition

A **Counter** is a PLC instruction that tallies the number of input transitions (events) and produces a Boolean output when the accumulated count reaches or exceeds a preset value. Counters are indispensable for batch-counting, position-event tracking, and process-cycle counting.

**KTU 2024 Standard Classification of PLC Counters:**

| Counter Type | IEC 61131-3 Symbol | Functional Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Count Up | **CTU** | Increments ACC on each CU pulse; Q=TRUE when ACC ≥ PV |
| Count Down | **CTD** | Decrements ACC on each CD pulse; Q=TRUE when ACC ≤ 0 |
| Count Up/Down | **CTUD** | Bidirectional counting via separate CU and CD inputs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Counter ACC value typically ranges from **0 to 32,767** in a 16-bit PLC. Exceeding this saturates the counter at its maximum (CTU) or underflows at zero (CTD).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

**Timer Analogy — The Egg Timer:**
Imagine boiling an egg. You twist the egg timer to 5 minutes; the sand starts falling the moment you start it (input TRUE), but the bell rings (output TRUE) ONLY after 5 full minutes. This is exactly how an **On-Delay Timer (TON)** behaves — it "delays the consequence."

**Counter Analogy — The Stadium Turnstile:**
Picture a stadium turnstile counting fans entering. Each person passing through adds +1 to the count. When the count hits the stadium's capacity (Preset Value), the gate locks (Output Q = TRUE), preventing further entry. This is exactly a **Count-Up Counter (CTU)**.

---

## 1.4 Temperature Control — Problem Context

**Temperature Control** in PLC automation refers to a closed-loop (or semi-closed) supervisory strategy where a PLC reads a temperature sensor (e.g., RTD, thermocouple via analog module), compares it against Set Point (SP), and energizes/de-energizes heating or cooling actuators (heaters, fans, solenoids) using **timer-based hysteresis** to prevent rapid cycling (chattering).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Term:** **Hysteresis Band (ΔT)** is the dead-band around the setpoint within which no control action occurs, used to prevent actuator wear. Typical industrial values: **2 °C to 5 °C**.

---

## 1.5 Valve Sequencing — Problem Context

**Valve Sequencing** refers to the deterministic, time-ordered or event-ordered opening/closing of multiple process valves (e.g., INLET, OUTLET, DRAIN, BYPASS) to safely execute a multi-stage batch process such as filling, mixing, draining, and cleaning. PLC timers and counters orchestrate these stages deterministically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Sequential Function Charts (SFC) or ladder logic with **latching flags (memory bits)** are the two industry-preferred methods for valve sequencing. Students must master both.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Timer Instruction Block

Every PLC timer block (e.g., in Allen-Bradley RSLogix, Siemens TIA Portal, or CODESYS) has three core operational parameters:

- **Enable Input (IN):** The Boolean condition that activates the timer.
- **Preset Value (PV):** The target time-count the timer must reach before setting its output.
- **Accumulated Value (ACC):** The current elapsed count, updated on each scan.

**Operating Logic of TON (On-Delay Timer):**

1. When **IN = FALSE** → ACC resets to **0**, Output **Q = FALSE**.
2. When **IN = TRUE** → Timer begins incrementing ACC every scan cycle at the resolution of the time base.
3. When **ACC ≥ PV** → Output **Q = TRUE** (latched as long as IN remains TRUE).
4. When **IN = FALSE** → ACC instantly resets; Q drops to FALSE.

---

## 2.2 Operating Logic of TOF (Off-Delay Timer)

1. When **IN = TRUE** → Q = TRUE instantly; ACC remains 0.
2. When **IN transitions from TRUE to FALSE** → ACC begins incrementing.
3. When **ACC ≥ PV** → Q drops to FALSE; ACC holds.
4. If **IN returns TRUE before ACC reaches PV** → ACC resets, Q stays TRUE.

---

## 2.3 Operating Logic of Retentive Timer (RTO)

The RTO is unique because **it does NOT reset ACC when IN goes FALSE**. It only resets when an explicit **RESET (RST)** coil is energized. This makes it ideal for **total run-time accumulation** in industrial machinery (e.g., motor bearing life tracking).

---

## 2.4 Anatomy of Counter Instruction Blocks

### CTU (Count Up Counter)

- **CU Input:** Rising-edge trigger; each FALSE→TRUE transition increments ACC by 1.
- **R (Reset):** When TRUE, ACC = 0 and Q = FALSE.
- **Q Output:** TRUE when **ACC ≥ PV**.

### CTUD (Count Up/Down Counter)

- **CU Input:** Increments ACC.
- **CD Input:** Decrements ACC (saturates at 0).
- **QU Output:** TRUE when **ACC ≥ PV**.
- **QD Output:** TRUE when **ACC ≤ 0**.
- **RESET:** Forces ACC = 0.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU 2024 HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elapsed Time | $T = PV \times T_b$ | seconds | $T_b$ = time base (1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s) |
| 2 | Preset from Time | $PV = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_b}$ | integer | Round UP to next integer |
| 3 | Counter ACC update | $ACC_{new} = ACC_{old} + 1$ (CTU) | integer | $0 \le ACC \le 32{,}767$ |
| 4 | Count-Down update | $ACC_{new} = ACC_{old} - 1$ (CTD) | integer | Saturated at 0 |
| 5 | Hysteresis Heating | $T_{ON} \text{ when } T < SP - \Delta T$ | °C | $\Delta T$ = hysteresis band |
| 6 | Hysteresis Cooling | $T_{OFF} \text{ when } T > SP + \Delta T$ | °C | Dead-band prevents chattering |
| 7 | Pulse Repetition | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | Hz | $T$ in seconds |
| 8 | Batch Quantity | $N_{batches} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{Q_{per\_batch}}$ | integer | Round DOWN |
| 9 | Scan Time Effect | $T_{accuracy} = ACC \pm 1 \text{ scan}$ | ms | Scan ≈ 10–50 ms typical |
| 10 | Total Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = \sum T_{stage,i}$ | s | Sequential stages |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` is unnecessary here since no absolute-value bars are used inside table cells — the pipe issue is avoided entirely.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Paint Shop:** PLC TON timers enforce minimum dwell time for primer curing before the next robotic arm moves.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** CTU counters track backwash cycles of filter beds, triggering cleaning after N liters processed.
- **Food & Beverage (F&B):** Valve sequencing ensures CIP (Clean-In-Place) follows exact fill → mix → drain → rinse cycles.
- **HVAC Systems:** Temperature control with hysteresis reduces compressor cycling by **up to 60%**, extending equipment life.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** Precise TON/TOF cascades ensure regulatory compliance for reaction dwell times.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — Temperature Control Using PLC

### Problem Statement
A chemical reactor must be maintained at **Set Point (SP) = 80 °C** with a **hysteresis band ΔT = ±5 °C**. A PT100 RTD sensor feeds an analog input module (scaled 0–10 V = 0–200 °C). Design a PLC ladder program using **ON/OFF control** with timers to:
- Energize HEATER when temperature falls below **75 °C**.
- De-energize HEATER when temperature exceeds **85 °C**.
- Use a **minimum ON-time of 3 seconds** and **minimum OFF-time of 2 seconds** to prevent actuator chattering.

### Step 1 — Define the Logic Variables

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `TEMP_LOW` | Sensor signal below 75 °C | Boolean |
| `TEMP_HIGH` | Sensor signal above 85 °C | Boolean |
| `HEATER` | Output to heater contactor | Boolean (Output) |
| `T_ON` | Minimum ON timer | TON, PV = 3 s |
| `T_OFF` | Minimum OFF timer | TOF, PV = 2 s |

### Step 2 — Convert Sensor Voltage to Temperature

The scaling equation (assuming linear RTD):

$$
T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{in}}{10\,\text{V}} \times 200\,^{\circ}C
$$

For the low threshold (75 °C):

$$
V_{LOW} = \dfrac{75}{200} \times 10 = 3.75\,\text{V}
$$

For the high threshold (85 °C):

$$
V_{HIGH} = \dfrac{85}{200} \times 10 = 4.25\,\text{V}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Timer Preset Values

Using $PV = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_b}$, and selecting $T_b = 100\,\text{ms}$:

For $T_{ON} = 3\,\text{s}$:

$$
PV_{ON} = \dfrac{3}{0.1} = 30
$$

For $T_{OFF} = 2\,\text{s}$:

$$
PV_{OFF} = \dfrac{2}{0.1} = 20
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Ladder Logic (Structured Text Equivalent)

**Rung 1:** Low-temperature detection

```
IF (AI_TEMP_INPUT <= 75.0) THEN TEMP_LOW := TRUE; ELSE TEMP_LOW := FALSE; END_IF;
```

**Rung 2:** High-temperature detection

```
IF (AI_TEMP_INPUT >= 85.0) THEN TEMP_HIGH := TRUE; ELSE TEMP_HIGH := FALSE; END_IF;
```

**Rung 3:** Minimum ON timer (TON)

```
T_ON(IN := TEMP_LOW, PT := T#3s);
```

**Rung 4:** Minimum OFF timer (TOF)

```
T_OFF(IN := NOT TEMP_HIGH, PT := T#2s);
```

**Rung 5:** Heater output (with both timer interlocks)

```
HEATER := T_ON.Q AND T_OFF.Q;
```

### Step 5 — State Transition Table

| Current State | TEMP_LOW | TEMP_HIGH | T_ON.Q | T_OFF.Q | HEATER |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T = 90 °C (above band) | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **OFF** |
| T = 78 °C (within band, was off) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **OFF** (waiting for OFF time) |
| T = 74 °C (below band) | 1 | 0 | 1 (after 3 s) | 1 | **ON** |
| T rises to 84 °C (still in band) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **ON** (anti-chatter lock) |
| T = 86 °C (above band) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1→0 (after 2 s) | **OFF** (after 2 s) |

### Step 6 — Simulation Verification

Run in CODESYS / TIA Portal / LogixPro:
1. Force `AI_TEMP_INPUT = 70.0` → Verify HEATER turns ON exactly 3 s after TEMP_LOW asserts.
2. Force `AI_TEMP_INPUT = 90.0` → Verify HEATER turns OFF exactly 2 s after TEMP_HIGH asserts.
3. Rapidly toggle between 70 and 90 → Verify HEATER does NOT chatter due to timer interlocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Without timers, the heater could toggle **hundreds of times per hour** in the hysteresis band, destroying the contactor. Timers extend contactor life by orders of magnitude.

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — Valve Sequencing in a Batch Mixing Tank

### Problem Statement
A pharmaceutical mixing tank has three pneumatically-actuated valves: **V1 (INLET)**, **V2 (DRAIN)**, and **V3 (BYPASS)**, plus a **MIXER MOTOR (M)**. The required cycle is:

| Stage | Action | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Fill | V1 OPEN, M OFF | Until level sensor `L1` = HIGH |
| 2. Mix | V1 CLOSED, M ON | 30 seconds |
| 3. Drain | V2 OPEN, M OFF | Until level sensor `L2` = LOW (tank empty) |
| 4. Purge | V3 OPEN | 10 seconds |
| 5. Idle | All OFF | Wait for START pushbutton |

**Design a PLC ladder program using a sequencer (sector-based memory bits).**

### Step 1 — Define Memory Flags (Sequencer Bits)

Use five internal `BOOL` memory bits — one per stage:

| Bit | Meaning | Active Stage |
|---|---|---|
| `S1` | Stage 1 (FILL) | 1 |
| `S2` | Stage 2 (MIX) | 2 |
| `S3` | Stage 3 (DRAIN) | 3 |
| `S4` | Stage 4 (PURGE) | 4 |
| `S5` | Stage 5 (IDLE) | 5 |

### Step 2 — Define I/O Allocation

| Address | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | `START_PB` | Start pushbutton (NO, momentary) |
| I0.1 | `STOP_PB` | Stop pushbutton (NC) |
| I0.2 | `L1` | High-level sensor (tank full) |
| I0.3 | `L2` | Low-level sensor (tank empty) |
| Q0.0 | `V1` | INLET valve solenoid |
| Q0.1 | `V2` | DRAIN valve solenoid |
| Q0.2 | `V3` | BYPASS valve solenoid |
| Q0.3 | `M` | MIXER motor contactor |

### Step 3 — Timer Preset Calculation

For **30 s mix time** with $T_b = 100\,\text{ms}$:

$$
PV_{mix} = \dfrac{30}{0.1} = 300
$$

For **10 s purge time** with $T_b = 100\,\text{ms}$:

$$
PV_{purge} = \dfrac{10}{0.1} = 100
$$

### Step 4 — Ladder Logic Construction (Pseudo-Code)

**Rung 1 — System Enable Latch:**

```
START_PB OR System_Run --------------------( SET System_Run )
STOP_PB ------------------------------------( RST System_Run )
```

**Rung 2 — Stage 1 Transition (Idle → Fill):**

```
System_Run AND S5 AND START_PB --------------( RST S5 )
                                              ( SET S1 )
```

**Rung 3 — Stage 1 Outputs:**

```
S1 ------------------------------------------( ) V1  (V1 OPEN)
NOT S1 --------------------------------------( / ) V1
```

**Rung 4 — Stage 1 → Stage 2 Transition (Fill complete):**

```
S1 AND L1 ----------------------------------( RST S1 )
                                              ( SET S2 )
                                              ( RST T_MIX )
```

**Rung 5 — Mix Timer (TON):**

```
S2 ------------------------------------------[TON T_MIX, PV=300 (30s)]
```

**Rung 6 — Stage 2 Outputs:**

```
S2 ------------------------------------------( ) M  (Mixer ON)
NOT S2 --------------------------------------( / ) M
```

**Rung 7 — Stage 2 → Stage 3 Transition (Mix complete):**

```
S2 AND T_MIX.Q -----------------------------( RST S2 )
                                              ( SET S3 )
```

**Rung 8 — Stage 3 Outputs (Drain):**

```
S3 ------------------------------------------( ) V2  (Drain OPEN)
NOT S3 --------------------------------------( / ) V2
```

**Rung 9 — Stage 3 → Stage 4 Transition (Drain complete):**

```
S3 AND NOT L2 -------------------------------( RST S3 )
                                              ( SET S4 )
                                              ( RST T_PURGE )
```

**Rung 10 — Purge Timer (TON):**

```
S4 ------------------------------------------[TON T_PURGE, PV=100 (10s)]
```

**Rung 11 — Stage 4 Outputs:**

```
S4 ------------------------------------------( ) V3  (Bypass OPEN)
NOT S4 --------------------------------------( / ) V3
```

**Rung 12 — Stage 4 → Stage 5 Transition (Purge complete):**

```
S4 AND T_PURGE.Q ----------------------------( RST S4 )
                                              ( SET S5 )
```

### Step 5 — State Transition Truth Table

| Current Stage | Trigger Event | Next Stage | Outputs Active |
|---|---|---|---|
| S5 (Idle) | START_PB pressed | **S1** (Fill) | V1 |
| S1 (Fill) | L1 = HIGH | **S2** (Mix) | M (after V1 closes) |
| S2 (Mix) | T_MIX.Q (30 s elapsed) | **S3** (Drain) | V2 |
| S3 (Drain) | L2 = LOW (tank empty) | **S4** (Purge) | V3 |
| S4 (Purge) | T_PURGE.Q (10 s elapsed) | **S5** (Idle) | None |
| Any Stage | STOP_PB pressed | **S5** (Idle) | All OFF |

### Step 6 — Simulation Steps

1. Open **CODESYS**, **LogixPro**, or **PLC Ladder Simulator**.
2. Enter the rungs above.
3. Force `START_PB = TRUE` → Observe V1 energizes.
4. Force `L1 = TRUE` → Observe V1 de-energizes, M energizes, T_MIX begins counting.
5. Wait 30 s → Observe M de-energizes, V2 energizes.
6. Force `L2 = FALSE` → Observe V2 de-energizes, V3 energizes, T_PURGE starts.
7. Wait 10 s → Observe V3 de-energizes, system returns to S5.
8. At any stage, force `STOP_PB = TRUE` → Verify immediate reset to S5.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Set/Reset (Latching) Coils?** Momentary pushbuttons cannot sustain a stage flag indefinitely. Latching flags create a "sticky" memory that persists until the transition condition explicitly resets them. This is the **industry-standard pattern** for sequential control.

---

## 3.3 Counter-Augmented Variant — Bottle-Filling Conveyor

**Problem:** A conveyor carries empty bottles. A sensor counts bottles. After every **12 bottles**, the conveyor stops for **5 seconds** to allow a tray-change operation. Design this using a **CTU counter**.

### Step 1 — Counter Preset

$$
PV_{counter} = 12 \text{ (bottles)}
$$

### Step 2 — Timer Preset (5 s stop)

$$
PV_{timer} = \dfrac{5}{0.1} = 50
$$

### Step 3 — Ladder Logic (Pseudo)

```
BOTTLE_SENSOR (rising edge) -----------------[CTU C1, PV=12]
                                              (RST via C1.Q and T_DONE.Q)
```

```
C1.Q ----------------------------------------[TON T_DONE, PV=50]
```

```
CONVEYOR_RUN := (NOT C1.Q) OR (C1.Q AND NOT T_DONE.Q)
```

### Step 4 — Behavior Trace

| Bottles Passed | C1.ACC | C1.Q | Conveyor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 to 11 | 0–11 | 0 | **RUN** |
| 12 | 12 | 1 | **STOP**, T_DONE starts |
| 12 (after 5 s) | — | 1→0 (after RST) | **RUN** again |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Operational State Machine (TON)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle : Power ON
    Idle --> Timing : IN = TRUE
    Timing --> Done : ACC >= PV
    Done --> Idle : IN = FALSE
    Done --> Timing : IN stays TRUE (Q remains TRUE)
    Timing --> Idle : IN = FALSE (Reset ACC)
    note right of Done : Output Q = TRUE here
    note left of Timing : ACC increments at time-base rate
```

---

## 4.2 Counter Operational State Machine (CTU)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ResetState
    ResetState --> Counting : CU rising edge
    Counting --> Counting : ACC < PV
    Counting --> TargetReached : ACC >= PV
    TargetReached --> ResetState : RST = TRUE
    TargetReached --> TargetReached : CU continues but ACC saturates
    note right of TargetReached : Q = TRUE, ACC clamped at PV or max
```

---

## 4.3 Temperature Control — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RTD PT100 Sensor] --> B[Analog Input Module AI4]
    B --> C[Scaling Block T C = V x 20]
    C --> D{Comparator SP 80C}
    D --> E[TEMP_LOW less than 75C]
    D --> F[TEMP_HIGH greater than 85C]
    E --> G[TON T ON PV 30 3 seconds]
    F --> H[TOF T OFF PV 20 2 seconds]
    G --> I[AND Gate]
    H --> I
    I --> J[HEATER Contactor Q0 0]
    J --> K[Heater Element]
    K --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Valve Sequencing — Block-Level Sequential Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    StartPB[Start Pushbutton I0 0] --> S5Set[Set S5 Idle]
    StopPB[Stop Pushbutton I0 1] --> ResetAll[Reset All Stages]
    S5Set --> S1[S1 Fill]
    S1 -->|L1 HIGH| S2[S2 Mix]
    S2 -->|T MIX 30 s Q TRUE| S3[S3 Drain]
    S3 -->|L2 LOW| S4[S4 Purge]
    S4 -->|T PURGE 10 s Q TRUE| S5Idle[S5 Idle]
    S1 --> V1Out[V1 INLET Solenoid Q0 0]
    S2 --> MOut[M MIXER Motor Q0 3]
    S3 --> V2Out[V2 DRAIN Solenoid Q0 1]
    S4 --> V3Out[V3 BYPASS Solenoid Q0 2]
```

---

## 4.5 Timer/Counter Interaction Pattern

```mermaid
graph TD
    In1[Input Pulse CU] --> CTU1[CTU Counter]
    CTU1 -->|Q TRUE when ACC greater than equal PV| TMR1[TON Timer]
    TMR1 -->|Q TRUE after delay| Out1[Output Action e g Valve Activate]
    RST[Reset Coil] --> CTU1
    RST --> TMR1
```

---

## 4.6 Bottle-Counting Conveyor — System Overview

```mermaid
graph LR
    Conv[Conveyor Motor] --> Bot[Bottle]
    Bot -->|Passes through| Sens[Photoelectric Sensor]
    Sens --> CTU2[CTU Counter PV 12]
    CTU2 -->|Q TRUE| StopRel[Stop Relay]
    StopRel --> TMR2[TON Timer 5 s]
    TMR2 -->|After 5 s| RST2[RST Counter]
    RST2 --> CTU2
    TMR2 -->|Q FALSE after timer| Conv
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the operating principle of an ON-Delay Timer (TON) in a PLC with the help of a timing diagram. Mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An **On-Delay Timer (TON)** is an instruction that delays the activation of its output by a preset duration after its input turns ON.

- When `IN = FALSE` → Accumulator (ACC) = 0; Output Q = FALSE.
- When `IN = TRUE` → Timer begins incrementing ACC at the time-base rate.
- When `ACC ≥ PV` → Q = TRUE (held as long as IN stays TRUE).
- If IN goes FALSE anytime → ACC resets to 0 instantly.

**Timing Diagram (textual):**

```
IN : _____-------____________________
ACC: __________----______________
Q  : _____________________----______
             ↑delay PV↑
```

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Star-Delta Motor Starter:** TON delays transition from star to delta by 5–10 seconds.
2. **Conveyor Belt Sequential Start:** Each downstream conveyor starts after a delay, preventing simultaneous inrush currents.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a Count-Up (CTU) and Count-Up/Down (CTUD) counter. Provide one practical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CTU | CTUD |
|---|---|---|
| Counting Direction | Increments only | Increments AND decrements |
| Inputs | CU, RESET | CU, CD, RESET |
| Outputs | Q (ACC ≥ PV) | QU (ACC ≥ PV), QD (ACC ≤ 0) |
| Typical Use | Batch counting | Parking lot occupancy tracking |

**Practical Examples:**
- **CTU:** Counting bottles on a filling line — increments per bottle.
- **CTUD:** Parking garage car counter — increments when a car enters, decrements when a car exits; QU triggers "FULL" sign.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set B]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Design and explain a PLC ladder program for a temperature control system where a furnace must maintain temperature at 200 °C with a hysteresis of ±10 °C. Use a TON timer to enforce a minimum heater ON-time of 10 seconds to prevent contactor wear. Show the I/O allocation, ladder logic, and timing diagram.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**I/O Allocation:**

| Address | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| AIW0 | TEMP_AI | Analog temperature input (scaled 0–500 °C) |
| I0.0 | START | Start pushbutton |
| I0.1 | EMERGENCY_STOP | Emergency stop (NC) |
| Q0.0 | HEATER | Heater contactor |
| T1 | T_MIN_ON | TON, PV = 100 (10 s with 100 ms base) |

**Scaling Equations:**

$$
T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{AIW0}{27648} \times 500
$$

**Logic Thresholds:**
- **LOW (190 °C):** Heater allowed ON
- **HIGH (210 °C):** Heater forced OFF

**Ladder Rungs:**

**Rung 1 — Enable Latch:**
```
START AND NOT EMERGENCY_STOP -------(SET SYSTEM_RUN)
EMERGENCY_STOP ---------------------(RST SYSTEM_RUN)
```

**Rung 2 — Low-Temp Compare:**
```
IF TEMP_AI <= 19000 THEN LOW_FLAG := TRUE;
```

**Rung 3 — High-Temp Compare:**
```
IF TEMP_AI >= 21000 THEN HIGH_FLAG := TRUE;
```

**Rung 4 — Minimum ON Timer:**
```
LOW_FLAG AND SYSTEM_RUN ------------[TON T_MIN_ON, PV=100]
```

**Rung 5 — Heater Output:**
```
T_MIN_ON.Q AND NOT HIGH_FLAG -------( ) HEATER
```

**Timing Diagram:**

```
TEMP : _____200_______________210____________
LOW  : __________-------____________________
HIGH : ______________________________----____
HEAT : __________---------------------------_
              ↑10 s min↑
```

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Extend the above program to include a minimum OFF-time of 15 seconds using a TOF timer. Explain how this prevents chattering. Also, simulate the program in LogixPro and tabulate the state changes.**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Additional Timer:**

| Timer | Type | PV | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| T_MIN_OFF | TOF | 150 (15 s) | Hold heater OFF for 15 s after temperature drops |

**Modified Rung 4:**
```
HIGH_FLAG -------------------------[TOF T_MIN_OFF, PV=150]
```

**Modified Rung 5:**
```
T_MIN_ON.Q AND T_MIN_OFF.Q --------( ) HEATER
```

**Chattering Prevention Explanation:**

Without minimum OFF-time, if the temperature oscillates around 200 °C (e.g., sensor noise), the heater would rapidly cycle ON/OFF. Each cycle stresses the contactor contacts. By enforcing a **15-second minimum OFF-time**, the contactor rests and cools, extending its mechanical life by **5–10×**.

**Simulation State Table (LogixPro):**

| Time (s) | TEMP (°C) | LOW_FLAG | HIGH_FLAG | T_MIN_ON.Q | T_MIN_OFF.Q | HEATER |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 180 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | OFF |
| 5 | 185 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | OFF |
| 10 | 188 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ON |
| 25 | 205 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ON |
| 28 | 212 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1→0 (15 s later) | OFF |
| 43 | 195 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | OFF (held) |
| 58 | 190 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0→1 | OFF→ON eligible |

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set A]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A bottling plant has three stations: (i) Filling, (ii) Capping, (iii) Labeling. Bottles move on a conveyor. After every 10 bottles, the conveyor stops for 8 seconds to allow label-roll replacement. Design a PLC program using a CTU counter and a TON timer. Provide I/O list, ladder logic, and explain the sequence.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**I/O Allocation:**

| Address | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START | Start pushbutton |
| I0.1 | STOP | Stop pushbutton (NC) |
| I0.2 | BOTTLE_SENSOR | Photoelectric sensor (rising edge) |
| I0.3 | LABEL_REPLACED | Operator confirmation PB |
| Q0.0 | CONVEYOR | Conveyor motor |
| Q0.1 | FILL_VALVE | Filling solenoid |
| Q0.2 | CAP_HEAD | Capping pneumatic cylinder |
| Q0.3 | LABEL_DISP | Label dispenser |
| C1 | BOTTLE_CTR | CTU, PV = 10 |
| T1 | STOP_DELAY | TON, PV = 80 (8 s) |

**Ladder Rungs:**

**Rung 1 — Counter Increment:**
```
BOTTLE_SENSOR (rising edge) -------[CTU C1, PV=10]
```

**Rung 2 — Counter Reset:**
```
LABEL_REPLACED -------------------(RST C1)
```

**Rung 3 — Delay Timer:**
```
C1.Q ----------------------------[TON STOP_DELAY, PV=80]
```

**Rung 4 — Conveyor Control:**
```
CONVEYOR := NOT C1.Q OR NOT STOP_DELAY.Q
```

**Rung 5 — Station Outputs (active during conveyor run):**
```
CONVEYOR -----------------------( ) FILL_VALVE
FILL_DONE ----------------------( ) CAP_HEAD
CAP_DONE -----------------------( ) LABEL_DISP
```

**Sequence Explanation:**

1. **Normal Run:** Conveyor runs; bottles pass sensor → C1 counts up.
2. **At 10th Bottle:** C1.Q = TRUE → Conveyor stops → T1 starts.
3. **During 8 s:** Operator replaces label roll and presses LABEL_REPLACED.
4. **Reset:** LABEL_REPLACED resets C1 → C1.Q = FALSE → Conveyor resumes.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For the same bottling plant, redesign using a CTUD counter to additionally count rejected bottles separately, where a quality sensor rejects 1 in 5 bottles. Show the modified counter logic and explain how the PLC computes the rejection rate dynamically.**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Modified Counter Setup:**

| Counter | Type | Function | Preset |
|---|---|---|---|
| C2 | CTU | Total bottles counted | No preset (free-running) |
| C3 | CTU | Rejected bottles | No preset |

**Additional Inputs:**

| Address | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| I0.4 | Q_SENSOR | Quality reject sensor (rising edge on defect) |

**Ladder Rungs:**

**Rung 1 — Total Count:**
```
BOTTLE_SENSOR (rising edge) -------[CTU C2]
```

**Rung 2 — Reject Count:**
```
Q_SENSOR (rising edge) -----------[CTU C3]
```

**Rung 3 — Rejection Rate Calculation (Analog Output or HMI Display):**
$$
\text{Rejection Rate (\%)} = \dfrac{C3.ACC}{C2.ACC} \times 100
$$

This is computed in the HMI/SCADA layer; the PLC only stores counts in its data registers (e.g., `MW10` for C2.ACC, `MW12` for C3.ACC).

**Rung 4 — Alarm Trigger:**
```
IF (C3.ACC * 20) >= C2.ACC THEN ALARM := TRUE;  // >5% reject rate
```

**Explanation:**
- PLC increments C2 on every bottle.
- PLC increments C3 only when a defect is detected.
- SCADA polls C2.ACC and C3.ACC and computes the rejection rate in real time.
- If rate exceeds 5%, an alarm (lamp or buzzer) is raised for the supervisor.

**Simulation Output Table:**

| Total (C2.ACC) | Rejects (C3.ACC) | Rejection Rate (%) | Alarm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 15 | 15% | **ON** |
| 200 | 20 | 10% | **ON** |
| 500 | 24 | 4.8% | OFF |
| 1000 | 18 | 1.8% | OFF |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to RESET counters/timers** between cycles → Expect -2 marks deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up TON and TOF semantics** in hysteresis loops → Common 3-mark loss.
> 3. **Not specifying the time base** in PV calculations → Examiner flags "incomplete answer."
> 4. **Omitting I/O allocation tables** → Direct loss of 1–2 marks for "lack of design rigor."
> 5. **Failing to include the timing diagram** → Up to 3-mark loss if the question explicitly asks.
> 6. **Not justifying the hysteresis band** → Examiner expects an engineering reason (chatter prevention).
> 7. **Confusing rising-edge vs. level-triggered** inputs for counters — counters MUST use rising-edge (one-shot) signals.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Key Takeaway |
|---|---|
| ⏱️ | **TON** delays ON; **TOF** delays OFF; **TP** generates a pulse; **RTO** retains across power cycles. |
| 🔢 | **CTU** increments only; **CTD** decrements only; **CTUD** is bidirectional. |
| 📐 | **Time formula:** $PV = T_{desired} / T_b$. Always round UP. |
| 🎯 | **Counters saturate** at 32,767 (16-bit); reset before overflow to prevent logic faults. |
| 🌡️ | **Hysteresis control** uses BOTH a turn-ON threshold and a turn-OFF threshold to prevent chattering. |
| 🔧 | **Minimum ON/OFF timers** dramatically extend contactor life — always include them in industrial designs. |
| 🔁 | **Sequencer pattern** uses SET/RST latches on stage bits (S1, S2, S3...) to enforce deterministic stage transitions. |
| 🚦 | **I/O allocation table** is MANDATORY in every KTU design answer — examiners grade on engineering discipline. |
| 📊 | **Timing diagrams** carry significant marks — always sketch them for TON/TOF/CTU questions. |
| 🖥️ | **Simulation tools:** LogixPro, CODESYS, TIA Portal, PLC Ladder Simulator — students should practice in at least one. |
| ⚡ | **Scan-time effect:** Timer accuracy = PV × T_b ± 1 scan; account for it in fast processes. |
| 🏭 | **Real-world fields:** F&B, Pharma, Water Treatment, Automotive, HVAC — all use these patterns. |
| 📚 | **Latching coils** (SET/RST) are essential for stage memory — never rely on momentary inputs alone. |
| 🔍 | **Edge detection** (one-shot / rising-edge) is mandatory for counter inputs — repeated TRUE would count only once. |

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — TIMERS, COUNTERS, TEMPERATURE CONTROL & VALVE SEQUENCING**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conveyor Belt Control and Control of a Process, PLC Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conveyor Belt Control and Control of a Process, PLC Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Conveyor Belt Control – Formal Definition

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, **Conveyor Belt Control** refers to the automated regulation of motorized conveyor systems used in manufacturing, packaging, material handling, and bottling plants, where a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** orchestrates the start/stop sequencing, speed regulation, direction reversal, fault detection, and inter-station synchronization of one or more conveyor belts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box – Conveyor Control:**
> A conveyor belt control system is an *electro-pneumatic / electro-mechanical assembly* whose operations are governed by a PLC executing a *ladder logic program* derived from the *IEC 61131-3* standard. The control logic typically governs **motor starters (DOL/Star-Delta)**, **proximity sensors**, **photo-electric sensors**, **limit switches**, and **actuator solenoids**.

### 1.2 Control of a Process – Formal Definition

**Process Control using PLC** refers to the closed-loop (or semi-closed loop) supervision of an industrial process—such as *liquid level control*, *temperature control*, *pressure control*, or *bottling plant operation*—where the PLC monitors analogue/digital inputs from sensors, executes a control algorithm (PID, ON-OFF, sequential), and commands final control elements like *valves, pumps, heaters, and motors*.

### 1.3 PLC Installation – Formal Definition

**PLC Installation** is the systematic engineering practice of *mounting, wiring, earthing, commissioning, and validating* a Programmable Logic Controller and its associated I/O modules, field devices, and Human Machine Interface (HMI) panels in an industrial cabinet, conforming to standards such as **IEC 61131-2**, **IS/IEC 61508**, and manufacturer-specific installation guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants for KTU Examinations:**
> - PLC Scan Cycle time: typically **10 ms – 50 ms** per 1 K of logic
> - Operating Temperature (Industrial PLC): **0 °C to 55 °C**
> - Supply Voltage Standard: **24 V DC** (sensors), **230 V AC / 415 V AC** (actuators)
> - Humidity Tolerance: **5 % to 95 %** non-condensing

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 – Conveyor Belt = Airport Baggage System**
> Imagine the conveyor belts at an airport. Bags enter at the check-in counter (input sensor triggers), move along belts, get routed to belts 2 or 3 (sequencer logic), and reach the correct flight (output actuator). A single brain (the PLC) decides *when each belt moves, stops, or reverses*. Without that brain, bags would collide and fall off.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 – Process Control = Auto-Rice Cooker**
> An automatic rice cooker senses the water level and temperature (sensors → PLC inputs), and decides whether to keep the heating coil ON or OFF (PLC outputs → heater). This is exactly how a PLC controls a tank-filling process or a bottling line: **sense → decide → act**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 – PLC Installation = Electrical Switchboard Wiring**
> Installing a PLC inside a control panel is like organizing the electrical wiring of a house distribution board. Every wire has a fixed destination, earthing is critical, ventilation (cooling) must be considered, and the layout must be documented for future maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

### 2.1 Conveyor Belt Control – Operational Logic Steps

A typical KTU-labelled **two-conveyor sequential control** operates on the following logical sequence:

- **Step 1 – Initialization (Cold Start)**: When the START pushbutton (PB) is pressed, the system latches the *Master Relay (MR)*.
- **Step 2 – First Conveyor (C1) Activation**: The PLC energizes contactor **K1**, starting conveyor 1.
- **Step 3 – Object Detection**: A photo-electric sensor **PE1** detects the object on C1.
- **Step 4 – Delay / Transfer**: After a programmed timer delay (e.g., 5 s), conveyor 1 stops and conveyor 2 (**K2**) starts.
- **Step 5 – End Detection**: Sensor **PE2** at the far end of C2 detects the object leaving.
- **Step 6 – Reset**: Conveyor 2 stops after the object is delivered; system awaits next START.
- **Step 7 – Emergency Stop (E-STOP)**: A normally-closed emergency pushbutton is wired in **hardwired series** with all contactors—ensuring safety even if PLC fails (fail-safe design).

### 2.2 Conveyor Belt – Why and How

| Logic Stage | "Why" (Purpose) | "How" (Mechanism) |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Latch Memory | Maintain state after momentary START press | PLC internal relay (M-coil) with self-holding contact |
| Interlock | Prevent simultaneous C1 & C2 conflict | Normally-closed contact of K1 in series with K2 coil |
| Timer (T1) | Allow transfer time between belts | TON (Timer ON-delay) instruction |
| Sensor Debounce | Avoid false triggers from noise | Software delay filter (10–50 ms) |
| E-STOP | Personnel safety, fail-safe | Hardware-wired NC contact in series with master |

### 2.3 Process Control – Closed-Loop Sequence

For a classical **Tank Level Control** system used in KTU lab exercises:

- **Sensing Stage**: Level transmitter (LT) gives **4–20 mA** signal proportional to tank level.
- **Signal Conditioning**: PLC analogue input module converts current to engineering units (e.g., 0–100 %).
- **Decision Stage**: PLC compares actual level with setpoint (SP).
- **Actuation Stage**: If level < SP, INLET valve opens; if level ≥ SP, INLET valve closes (ON-OFF control) **OR** valve opens proportionally (PID control).
- **Alarm Stage**: High-High (HH) and Low-Low (LL) alarms trigger indicator lamps / sirens.

### 2.4 PLC Installation – Engineering Workflow

- **Phase 1 – Site Survey**: Ambient temperature, dust, vibration, EMI sources.
- **Phase 2 – Panel Design**: Layout drawing (CPU top, I/O modules bottom, PS at left).
- **Phase 3 – Mounting**: DIN-rail mounting on **35 mm** standard rail inside an IP-54/IP-65 enclosure.
- **Phase 4 – Wiring**:
    - Power Supply (L, N, E) – 230 V AC mains
    - CPU power terminals
    - Digital Inputs – typically 24 V DC sourcing/sinking
    - Digital Outputs – Relay (230 V AC / 2 A) or Transistor (24 V DC / 0.5 A)
    - Analogue Inputs – shielded twisted pair (STP)
- **Phase 5 – Earthing**: Dedicated earth bus bar with resistance **≤ 1 Ω**.
- **Phase 6 – Commissioning**: I/O check (force table), program download, dry run, then loaded test.
- **Phase 7 – Documentation**: Wiring diagram, ladder listing, I/O address list, backup of program on EPROM/SD card.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Configuration Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet for KTU 2024 – PLC Automation Module 3**

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------------------|
| Analogue Scaling (Current → Engineering) | $E = \dfrac{(I - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \times (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | $I$ in mA, $E$ in engineering units |
| Voltage → Current (Sensor Output) | $V = I \times R_{\text{burden}}$ | $R_{\text{burden}}$ typically **250 Ω** for 4–20 mA → 1–5 V |
| PID Output (Position Form) | $u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int e(t) dt + K_{d} \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $e(t) = SP - PV$ |
| ON-OFF Hysteresis Band | $\Delta H = SP_{\text{high}} - SP_{\text{low}}$ | Prevents relay chattering |
| Timer Preset Calculation | $T_{\text{preset}} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Delay}}{\text{Time Base}}$ | Time base: **0.01 s**, **0.1 s**, **1 s** |
| Counter Preset | $N_{\text{preset}} = \text{Total objects to count}$ | Integer ≥ 1 |
| Scan Time Approximation | $T_{\text{scan}} \approx \text{Overhead} + \sum (I/O \; \text{scan} + \text{Logic execution})$ | Typical: **10 ms – 50 ms** |
| Heat Dissipation in Panel | $P_{\text{diss}} = \sum V_{\text{drop}} \times I_{\text{load}}$ | Watts, dictates cooling fan sizing |
| Earth Resistance (Industrial) | $R_{\text{earth}} \leq 1 \; \Omega$ | IS 3043 standard |
| Minimum Creepage Distance (PCB) | $\geq 8 \; \text{mm}$ for 230 V AC | IEC 61131-2 |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Automotive assembly lines, FMCG packaging lines (Hindustan Unilever, Nestlé).
- **Process Industries**: Oil & Gas (ONGC), Water Treatment (KWA – Kerala Water Authority).
- **Smart Factories**: Industry 4.0 integration with SCADA / IoT dashboards.
- **Kerala Context**: Cochin Shipyard uses PLC-driven conveyor systems for steel plate movement; BHEL Trichy uses PLC process control for boiler drum level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Analogue Signal Scaling (4–20 mA → 0–100 %)

Industrial transmitters universally use the **4–20 mA current loop** because *4 mA represents "zero" (live zero)* and *20 mA represents "full scale"*. The PLC's analogue input module sees a voltage proportional to current across a burden resistor.

**Step 1** — Express the linear relationship between current $I$ and engineering value $E$.

By definition of linearity, $E$ is a linear function of $I$:

$$
E = a \cdot I + b
$$

**Step 2** — Apply boundary conditions.

When $I = 4 \; \text{mA}$, then $E = E_{\min}$.

$$
E_{\min} = a \cdot 4 + b
$$

When $I = 20 \; \text{mA}$, then $E = E_{\max}$.

$$
E_{\max} = a \cdot 20 + b
$$

**Step 3** — Solve for the slope $a$.

Subtract the two equations to eliminate $b$:

$$
E_{\max} - E_{\min} = a \cdot (20 - 4)
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{16}
$$

**Step 4** — Solve for the intercept $b$.

Substitute back:

$$
b = E_{\min} - 4 \cdot \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{16}
$$

$$
b = E_{\min} - \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{4}
$$

**Step 5** — Combine into the final scaling formula.

$$
E = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{16} \cdot (I - 4) + E_{\min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> A level transmitter has a range 0–5 metres. It outputs 4–20 mA. If the measured current is **12 mA**, calculate the tank level.
>
> Using $E_{\min} = 0$, $E_{\max} = 5$, $I = 12$:
>
> $$
> E = \dfrac{5 - 0}{16} \cdot (12 - 4) + 0
> $$
>
> $$
> E = \dfrac{5}{16} \cdot 8 = 2.5 \; \text{metres}
> $$

### 3.2 Derivation of PID Controller Output (Standard Text-Book Form)

The PID algorithm is essential for KTU viva-voce questions.

**Step 1** — Define the error signal as the difference between Setpoint (SP) and Process Variable (PV).

$$
e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)
$$

**Step 2** — Express the proportional term.

$$
P_{\text{out}} = K_{p} \cdot e(t)
$$

**Step 3** — Express the integral term (sum of past errors).

$$
I_{\text{out}} = K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 4** — Express the derivative term (rate of change of error).

$$
D_{\text{out}} = K_{d} \cdot \dfrac{d e(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 5** — Combine all three into the final controller output.

$$
u(t) = K_{p} \, e(t) + K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_{d} \, \dfrac{d e(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 6** — Digital (discrete) form for PLC implementation (sampling time $T_{s}$).

$$
u[k] = K_{p} \, e[k] + K_{i} \, T_{s} \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + K_{d} \, \dfrac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_{s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Windup Note**: If $u(t)$ saturates the actuator, the integrator must be "frozen" to avoid integral windup — a common viva question.

### 3.3 Derivation of Timer Preset from Engineering Requirement

**Given**: A conveyor transfer delay of **7.5 seconds** is required.
**Time base** chosen in PLC: **0.1 s** per count.

$$
T_{\text{preset}} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Delay (s)}}{\text{Time Base (s/count)}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{preset}} = \dfrac{7.5}{0.1} = 75 \; \text{counts}
$$

Thus the TON timer must be programmed with preset value **75** and time base **0.1 s**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Common KTU Industrial Scenarios

| Application | Input Devices | Output Devices | Control Strategy | Key Timer/Counter |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Bottle Filling Plant | Level switch, Proximity sensor | Solenoid valve, Conveyor motor, Indicator lamp | Sequential + ON-OFF | TON for filling time, CTU for bottle count |
| Two-Belt Conveyor | PE sensor (×2), Limit switch | Contactor (×2), Buzzer | Latching + Interlock | TON for transfer delay |
| Tank Level Control | Level transmitter (4–20 mA) | Inlet pump / solenoid | PID or ON-OFF with hysteresis | Sample time $T_{s} = 0.5 \; \text{s}$ |
| Traffic Light (Bonus) | None (internal clock) | Red, Yellow, Green lamps | Sequential timer cascade | TON × 3 cascaded |
| Elevator Control | Floor limit switches (×n), Call PB (×n) | Motor contactor (Up/Down), Door motor | Latching + Priority logic | TON for door dwell time |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Design of Two-Conveyor Ladder Logic (KTU Lab Model)

**Step 1** — Define I/O address allocation.

| Device | Symbol | Address |
|--------|--------|---------|
| START PB (NO) | PB1 | I0.0 |
| STOP PB (NC) | PB2 | I0.1 |
| E-STOP (NC) | ES1 | I0.2 |
| Photo Sensor 1 | PE1 | I0.3 |
| Photo Sensor 2 | PE2 | I0.4 |
| Conveyor 1 Contactor | K1 | Q0.0 |
| Conveyor 2 Contactor | K2 | Q0.1 |
| Indicator Lamp | HL1 | Q0.2 |
| Master Relay | MR | M0.0 |
| Timer (Transfer delay) | T1 | T37 |

**Step 2** — Draw Rung 1: Master Latching.

$$
\text{Input: PB1 (NO)} \quad | \quad \text{MR (NO self-hold)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{MR coil (M0.0)}
$$

**Step 3** — Draw Rung 2: Conveyor 1 Run (with interlocks).

$$
\text{MR (NO)} \; | \; \overline{\text{PB2}} \; | \; \overline{\text{ES1}} \; | \; \overline{\text{K2 (NC interlock)}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{K1 (Q0.0)}
$$

**Step 4** — Draw Rung 3: Sensor-triggered transfer logic.

$$
\overline{\text{PE1}} \; | \; \overline{\text{K1 (NC)}} \; | \; T1_{\text{done}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{K2 (Q0.1)}
$$

**Step 5** — Draw Rung 4: Timer activation.

$$
\overline{\text{K1 (NC)}} \; | \; \text{PE1 (NO)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{T1 (TON, PT = 75 @ 0.1 s)}
$$

**Step 6** — Draw Rung 5: Indicator Lamp ON when system active.

$$
\text{MR (NO)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HL1 (Q0.2)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Two-Conveyor Sequential Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PB1[Start PB] --> MR1[Master Relay M0.0]
    PB2[Stop PB NC] --> MR1
    ES1[E Stop NC] --> MR1
    MR1 --> K1[Conveyor 1 Contactor K1]
    PE1[Photo Sensor PE1] --> T1[Timer T37 TON 75 x 0.1s]
    K1 --> T1
    T1 --> K2[Conveyor 2 Contactor K2]

    MR1 --> HL1[Indicator Lamp HL1]
    K2 -->|Reset| MR1
```

flowchart LR
    LT[Level Transmitter 4 to 20 mA] --> AI[PLC Analogue Input Module]

    AI --> CPU

```

    S4 --> S5[Digital Input Wiring 24V DC from Sensors]

    S6 --> S7[Analogue Wiring Shielded Twisted Pair]
    S7 --> S8[Earthing Busbar Resistance less than 1 Ohm]
    S8 --> S9[Program Download and IO Force Test]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid I/O Architecture Map – Typical Industrial PLC Panel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[Power Supply 230V AC to 24V DC] --> CPU[CPU Module]
    CPU --> DI[Digital Input Module 16 Ch]
    CPU --> DO[Digital Output Module 16 Ch Relay]
    CPU --> AI[Analogue Input Module 8 Ch]
    CPU --> AO[Analogue Output Module 4 Ch]
    DI --> SENS[Field Sensors PB Limit Switch PE Sensor]
    DO --> ACT[Contactors Solenoids Lamps Buzzers]
    AI --> TX[Transmitters Level Pressure Temp]
    AO --> VALVE[Control Valves VFD Speed Reference]
    CPU <--> HMI[Human Machine Interface HMI Panel]
    CPU <--> NET[Ethernet RS485 Profibus Network]
```
