# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Availability of a Redundant DCS Controller Pair (1oo2)

Consider a DCS controller pair with identical primary and backup units running in **hot-standby (synchronous redundancy)**.

**Step 1 — Define parameters.**
Let
- $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures per hour) of one controller.
- $\mu = 1 / T_{repair}$ = repair rate (repairs per hour).
- $MTBF = 1 / \lambda$, $MTTR = 1 / \mu$.

**Step 2 — Failure rate of redundant pair.**
For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) system, the system fails **only when both** controllers fail. Assuming independent failures with constant rate:

$$
\lambda_{system}^{1oo2} = \lambda^2 \cdot t \quad \text{(approximation for very small } t \text{)}
$$

More precisely, using Markov reliability:

$$
MTBF_{1oo2} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation justification.**
For two parallel identical components with failure rate $\lambda$, the combined $MTBF$ is obtained by integrating the reliability function:

$$
R_{1oo2}(t) = 2 e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

$$
MTBF_{1oo2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{1oo2}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(2 e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
MTBF_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \left[\dfrac{-e^{-2\lambda t}}{2\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty}
$$

$$
MTBF_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda} - 0\right) - \left(\dfrac{1}{2\lambda} - 0\right) = \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Availability.**

$$
A_{1oo2} = \dfrac{MTBF_{1oo2}}{MTBF_{1oo2} + MTTR} = \dfrac{3/(2\lambda)}{3/(2\lambda) + 1/\mu}
$$

Let $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ (small). Then:

$$
A_{1oo2} \approx 1 - \dfrac{2 \rho^2}{3} \quad \text{(for low failure rates)}
$$

This shows that **redundancy dramatically increases availability** — a 1oo2 system has availability $>0.999999$ in most process plants, equivalent to a few minutes of downtime per year.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PFD and SIL Mapping (1oo1 SIS)

A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of three subsystems: **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element**.

**Step 1 — Simplification.**
For a 1oo1 (single-channel, no redundancy) SIF with proof test interval $T_{proof}$ and dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\right) dt
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the exponential.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \left(1 - \left[1 - \lambda_D t + \dfrac{(\lambda_D t)^2}{2!} - \cdots \right]\right) dt
$$

**Step 3 — Truncate at first order (valid since $\lambda_D t \ll 1$).**

$$
PFD_{avg} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_D t \, dt = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_{proof}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}^2}{2} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — SIL mapping.**
If $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr (1 year):

$$
PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
$$

This lies in the **SIL-1 band** ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$). For SIL-3, we need PFD $\le 10^{-3}$, which can be achieved by either (a) shorter proof-test interval, or (b) 1oo2 / 2oo3 redundancy in the SIF.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Network Latency and Control Loop Stability

For a control loop with proportional gain $K_p$ and integral time $T_i$, the **maximum tolerable latency** (one-way, sensor-to-actuator) is empirically:

$$
t_{latency}^{max} \le \dfrac{T_{loop}}{10}
$$

where $T_{loop}$ is the loop execution period (scan time).

**Example:** For a temperature loop with $T_{loop} = 1$ s, $t_{latency}^{max} = 100$ ms. For a fast pressure loop with $T_{loop} = 100$ ms, $t_{latency}^{max} = 10$ ms — pushing us toward **deterministic Ethernet (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT, TSN)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Redundancy Topologies

| Topology | Hardware Cost | Availability vs. Single | Typical Use in DCS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simplex (1oo1) | $1 \times$ | $A$ | Non-critical loops |
| 1oo2 (parallel) | $2 \times$ | $A_{1oo2} \approx 1 - (1-A)^2$ | Critical controllers |
| 2oo2 (both must work) | $2 \times$ | $A^2$ (lower!) | Used for fail-safe shutdown |
| 2oo3 (two of three) | $3 \times$ | Very high, with diagnostics | ESD systems, turbine control |
| 1oo2D (diagnosed) | $2 \times$ + diagnostics | High, with auto-fail-to-repair | Modern SIS |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume 2oo2 is "more available" than 1oo1. It is NOT — it is *less* available but provides higher *safety integrity* (both must fail to cause a dangerous failure). Always clarify **availability vs. safety**.

> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows show *information flow*, not direct data paths. The DMZ (Level 3.5) is the **only** legal crossing point between operational and enterprise networks — a unidirectional gateway or data diode is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> The dashed arrows from PLC and DCS to SIS mean the SIS **monitors** the process but its outputs go **directly to final elements** (valves, breakers) — bypassing the DCS. This is the cornerstone of IEC 61511 independence.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define DCS and list any four "other key issues" that affect its reliable operation in a process plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is a digitally integrated network of geographically distributed controllers, I/O subsystems, HMIs, and engineering stations that perform real-time control and monitoring of an industrial process.

Four "other key issues" affecting DCS reliability are:
1. **Integration with PLCs, SCADA, and SIS** — protocol mismatch and inter-system latency.
2. **Communication redundancy and network faults** — single network failure can halt plant.
3. **Cybersecurity threats** — viruses, ransomware, and insider attacks (e.g., Stuxnet).
4. **Alarm flooding and poor operator-interface design** — leading to operator errors.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and brief justification of 4 issues.)*

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**State the Purdue Reference Model and explain the role of the DMZ (Level 3.5) in DCS cybersecurity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95/IEC 62443)** divides an industrial automation network into 6 hierarchical levels, from Level 0 (field devices) to Level 5 (external internet/cloud). The **DMZ at Level 3.5** is a demilitarised zone that acts as a **buffer between operational technology (OT) at Level 3 and enterprise IT at Level 4**.

Its roles are:
- Prevent direct TCP/IP connectivity between corporate network and plant network.
- Force all data to pass through **firewalls, anti-virus, and data diodes** before crossing.
- Allow **unidirectional replication** (push from OT to IT) for historian, but block reverse intrusion.
- Mitigate attacks like Stuxnet/Triton that bridged the air-gap improperly.

*(1 mark for model, 2 marks for DMZ role.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **Purdue Reference Model** for industrial control system security. Discuss how the DMZ level prevents cyber-attacks on the DCS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **DCS and SCADA**. Explain how a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is integrated with a DCS while maintaining its independence as per IEC 61511. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Purdue Reference Model (7 marks):**

The **Purdue Reference Model** (also called the **ISA-95** or **Perdue/Williams** model) classifies industrial network architecture into 6 levels:

| Level | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Physical process | Sensors, valves, motors |
| 1 | Basic control | DCS controllers, PLCs, RTUs |
| 2 | Supervisory control | HMI, SCADA, alarm servers |
| 3 | Operations | MES, historians, asset management |
| 3.5 | **DMZ** | Firewalls, anti-virus, jump servers |
| 4 | Enterprise | ERP, email, file servers |
| 5 | External | Internet, cloud, vendor remote access |

**Role of DMZ (Level 3.5):**
- Provides **logical isolation** between OT and IT networks.
- Implements **firewall rules** that drop any unsolicited traffic from Level 4 reaching Level 3.
- Uses **data diodes** for unidirectional flow of historian data upwards.
- **Mandatory air-gap enforcement** prevents lateral movement of malware (e.g., Stuxnet used USB + shared printer to cross).
- Allows **patch and AV updates** to be staged at DMZ, then pulled into OT after validation.

*(3 marks: block diagram; 2 marks: explanation; 2 marks: DMZ role + example.)*

**(b) DCS vs SCADA + SIS Integration (7 marks):**

| Parameter | DCS | SCADA |
|---|---|---|
| Primary function | Closed-loop continuous control | Open-loop monitoring & supervisory |
| Architecture | Distributed controllers, plant-wide | Master-station, RTU-based, geographically spread |
| Data acquisition | High speed, deterministic | Polled, can be slower |
| Redundancy | Built-in (controllers, I/O, network) | Less inherent |
| Typical industry | Refineries, power plants, chemicals | Water, electrical grid, pipelines |

**SIS Integration with DCS (IEC 61511):**
- SIS is a **separate, independent** protection layer, certified to **SIL 1/2/3**.
- It uses **its own sensors, its own logic solver (Triconex/HIMA), and its own final elements**.
- The DCS **may provide input** to the SIS (e.g., process variable, command) but the SIS does **not** depend on DCS for actuation.
- If the DCS fails, the SIS **must still trigger** shutdown, isolation, or vent commands.
- The two systems share a **common cause failure analysis** to ensure true independence (separate power, separate cable trays, separate firmware teams).
- Communication between them is typically via **hard-wired I/O** rather than shared bus, to avoid common-mode network failure.

*(3 marks: DCS/SCADA table; 2 marks: SIS independence; 2 marks: IEC 61511 compliance explanation.)*

---

### Question B: **[Internal Choice to Question A]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **MTBF, MTTR, and Availability** in the context of DCS controller redundancy. A DCS controller pair operates with failure rate $\lambda = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate the **availability of a 1oo2 redundant pair** and comment on the improvement over a simplex system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **ISA 18.2 / EEMUA 191 alarm management lifecycle**. List the major reasons for **alarm flooding** in a DCS and the engineering remedies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) MTBF, MTTR, Availability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Definitions:**
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures — average operational time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair — average downtime to restore a failed component.
- **Availability** $A$ = fraction of time the system is functional:

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**For simplex (single controller):**

$$
MTBF = 1 / \lambda = 1 / (1 \times 10^{-5}) = 100{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
A_{simplex} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 8} = 0.99992 \quad \text{(i.e., 99.992%)}
$$

**For 1oo2 redundant pair:**

$$
MTBF_{1oo2} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 150{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
A_{1oo2} = \dfrac{150{,}000}{150{,}000 + 8} \approx 0.9999466
$$

**Improvement:**

$$
\Delta A = A_{1oo2} - A_{simplex} = 0.9999466 - 0.99992 = 2.66 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Downtime reduction: from $\approx 7$ hr/year to $\approx 4.7$ hr/year — a **33% reduction**.

*(2 marks: definitions; 2 marks: simplex calc; 2 marks: 1oo2 calc; 1 mark: comment.)*

**(b) Alarm Management Lifecycle (ISA 18.2) (7 marks):**

The **ISA 18.2 standard** defines a **seven-stage lifecycle** for managing process alarms:

1. **Alarm Philosophy** — document the site's alarm objectives, prioritisation scheme, and performance targets.
2. **Identification** — for every process deviation, identify the cause, consequence, response time, and operator action.
3. **Rationalisation** — review each alarm for validity, priority (1–3), and acceptable delay; remove nuisance, redundant, or stale alarms.
4. **Detailed Design** — define alarm dead-bands, shelving logic, on-off delay, and routing.
5. **Implementation** — configure the DCS, HMI graphics, and alarm-shelving policies.
6. **Operation** — perform real-time alarm handling, override, and acknowledgement training.
7. **Monitoring & Audit** — track KPIs like alarms-per-day, stale-alarm rate, and operator-alarm-load.

**EEMUA 191 Benchmark Targets:**
- $\le 150$ alarms per operator per day.
- $\le 5$ high-priority alarms per hour.
- $\le 1$ alarm per 10 minutes (acceptable operator load).

**Reasons for Alarm Flooding:**
- Improper tuning of process loops (PV oscillates across setpoint).
- Cascade failures of equipment.
- Poor engineering — single sensor loss triggering multiple derived alarms.
- Inadequate shelving during maintenance.

**Remedies:**
- Alarm rationalisation workshops (every 1–2 years).
- Dead-band and on-delay configuration.
- Use of **state-based alarming** (e.g., suppress pump-cavitation alarm when pump is off).
- Operator training and KPI dashboards.

*(3 marks: lifecycle; 2 marks: flooding causes; 2 marks: remedies + EEMUA numbers.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Availability with Safety** — high availability ≠ safe system. 2oo2 reduces availability but increases safety.
> 2. **Do not place SIS inside DCS scope** in diagrams — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for violating IEC 61511 independence.
> 3. **Do not write "DCS = SCADA"** — they are architecturally different (DCS = control + monitoring, SCADA = mostly monitoring).
> 4. **In availability calculations**, always show units and final % form (e.g., 99.992%), not just decimals.
> 5. **Memorise the EEMUA 191 numbers** (150/day, 5/hour) — KTU loves to ask these as 2–3 mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DCS** = Distributed Control System: plant-wide, closed-loop, distributed controllers, HMIs, engineering stations.
- **SCADA** ≠ DCS: SCADA is geographically wide, mostly supervisory, no closed-loop control.
- **SIS** is **separate** from DCS as per **IEC 61511** — independent hardware, logic, and power.
- **SIL levels (IEC 61508):** SIL-1 ($10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$), SIL-2 ($10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$), SIL-3 ($10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$).
- **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$; **1oo2 redundant** $MTBF = 3/(2\lambda)$.
- **PFD for 1oo1 SIF** $\approx \lambda_D T_{proof} / 2$.
- **Purdue Model** has **6 levels + DMZ (3.5)** — DMZ is the **only** legal bridge between OT and IT.
- **Major cyber-incidents:** Stuxnet (2010), Triton/TRISIS (2017) — both crossed the OT-IT boundary.
- **Alarm management** governed by **ISA 18.2** and **EEMUA 191**: target ≤ 150 alarms/day/operator.
- **Fieldbus protocols:** HART, Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus, Modbus, Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, OPC UA.
- **Network latency** for control: $t_{latency} \le T_{loop} / 10$.
- **Redundancy topologies:** 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D — know the difference between *availability* and *safety* optimisation.
- **Grounding rule:** single-point for low-frequency analog; **shielded cable grounded at one end** to avoid ground loops.
- **Cable segregation:** power cables > 30 cm from signal cables; analog > 15 cm from digital.
- **Lifecycle of DCS:** typical 15–20 years; migration options include IOU (I/O Upgrade) and full system migration.
- **OPC UA** is the modern replacement for OPC DA — provides **platform-independent, secure (TLS)**, information modelling.
- **KTU-favourite exam topics:** (1) DCS vs SCADA, (2) SIS independence, (3) Purdue model + DMZ, (4) availability calculation, (5) EEMUA 191 numbers, (6) PFD and SIL mapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Memory Aid for KTU Viva/Exam:**
> *"DCS = Control. SCADA = Monitor. SIS = Protect (independent). DMZ = Shield. ISA 18.2 = Manage Alarms. 1oo2 = Better Availability. 2oo3 = Better Safety."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging and Power System Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET832 - PLC AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Packaging and Power System Issues ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition — "Packaging" in the PLC Context > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):** > **Packaging** in a PLC system refers to the **physical enclosure, mounting configuration, and environmental protection standards** that house the processor, power supply, input/output (I/O) modules, and communication interfaces. It is the **mechanical and electrical containment strategy** that determines how a PLC is installed, cooled, grounded, and protected against industrial hazards such as dust, moisture, vibration, EMI (Electromagnetic Interference), and temperature extremes. In the KTU 2024 scheme, packaging is treated as a **deterministic design choice**, not an aesthetic one. The wrong package for an environment (e.g., an open-frame PLC in a foundry) results in **field failure rates of up to 40 % within the first 12 months**, according to industrial reliability surveys. The common packaging families are: | Form Factor | Typical Use | KTU Identifier | |---|---|---| | **Rack / Chassis Mount** | Large I/O counts (> 256), central control cabinets | Type-R | | **DIN-Rail Mount** | Mid-range distributed I/O, sub-panels | Type-D | | **Panel Mount / Wall Mount** | Compact machines, OEM skid integration | Type-P | | **Open Frame / Board-Level** | OEM-embedded, custom enclosures only | Type-O | ### 1.2 Definition — "Power System Issues" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **Power System Issues** in a PLC installation refer to the entire chain of phenomena affecting the quality, continuity, and integrity of electrical power delivered to the controller — including **voltage sags/swells, transients, harmonics, EMI/RFI noise, improper grounding, and backup-power availability**. The associated engineering discipline of **power conditioning** ensures that the PLC receives power within the manufacturer's published tolerance window (typically **±10 % of nominal** for steady-state voltage and **< 5 % THD** for harmonic content). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of the PLC as a human brain operating inside a factory.** The *packaging* is the **skull and skin** — it protects the brain from rain, dust, heat, and physical knocks. The *power system* is the **bloodstream** — even a momentary drop in oxygen (a voltage sag) makes the brain (CPU) "black out," causing a process to crash, scrap a batch, or in safety-critical systems, endanger people. A hospital (the plant) therefore installs **oxygen backup tanks (UPS), filters (line filters), surge protectors (TVS diodes), and proper plumbing (single-point grounding)** to keep the brain alive. That is *exactly* the engineering problem this module solves — only the "brain" is a **24 V DC CPU** and the "oxygen" is **120/230 V AC mains**. ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following are **non-negotiable benchmarks** for KTU-level answers: - **Standard PLC supply voltages:** **24 V DC** (most common for I/O), **120/230 V AC** (for CPU/PSU), **±10 % tolerance** steady-state - **Operating temperature range (industrial):** **0 °C to +60 °C** (standard), **−40 °C to +85 °C** (extended) - **Relative humidity:** **5 % to 95 % non-condensing** - **Ingress Protection (IP) rating floor for industrial floors:** **IP54** minimum, **IP65** for wash-down - **Dielectric withstand:** **1500 V AC for 1 minute** (UL 508) - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** typically **> 300,000 hours** for industrial-grade PLCs - **Standard THD limit for PLC PSU input:** **< 5 %** per IEEE 519 - **Surge withstand capability (IEEE C62.41):** **6 kV / 3 kA, 8/20 µs waveform** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of PLC Packaging **Pillar 1 — Mechanical Mounting** PLC packages are designed to mount on one of three standard structures: - **19-inch rack (IEC 60297)** — for centralized architecture - **35 mm DIN rail (EN 50022 / EN 50035)** — for distributed architecture - **Panel cut-out (NEMA cut-out dimensions)** — for machine-integrated OEM units > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for *naming* the rail standard (EN 50022 for top-hat 35 mm, EN 50035 for G-type 32 mm) when discussing modular PLC mounting. **Pillar 2 — Environmental Sealing (IP / NEMA)** The **IP code** (IEC 60529) is a two-digit number: - **First digit (0–6):** solid particle protection - **Second digit (0–9):** liquid ingress protection | IP Rating | Meaning | Typical PLC Location | |---|---|---| | **IP20** | Finger-safe, no water protection | Inside sealed control cabinet | | **IP54** | Dust-protected, splash-proof | Factory floor, machine side | | **IP65** | Dust-tight, jet-proof | Food/beverage wash-down | | **IP67** | Immersion to 1 m | Outdoor, submerged sensors | **Pillar 3 — Thermal Management** Heat is dissipated by three mechanisms: 1. **Conduction** through the backplane/mounting surface 2. **Natural convection** across the enclosure 3. **Forced air cooling** (fan-assisted, used when P_dissipated > 50 W) **Pillar 4 — Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** The package must act as a **Faraday cage** with: - Continuous **gaskets** (finger-stock or knitted mesh) on every seam - **Bonded** panel-to-door jumpers (low-impedance < 2.5 mΩ) - Conductive **zinc-rich or chromate coatings** on the inside surface **Pillar 5 — Vibration & Shock Resistance** Industrial PLCs are tested per **IEC 60068-2-6** (vibration, 10–500 Hz, 2 g) and **IEC 60068-2-27** (shock, 15 g / 11 ms). The package uses: - **Locking connectors** (not friction-fit) - **Spring-clamp terminals** (vibration-proof, no re-torque) - **Captive screws** (cannot fall into machinery) ### 2.2 The Five Power System Issues in a PLC Installation | # | Issue | Engineering Definition | Industrial Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Voltage Sag (Dip)** | RMS voltage drop of 10–90 % for 0.5 cycles to 1 min | **Most common** — 70 % of PLC power events | | 2 | **Transient / Surge** | Sub-cycle over-voltage (microseconds) from inductive switching or lightning | High — every motor contactor switching | | 3 | **Harmonic Distortion** | Non-sinusoidal currents from VFDs, SMPS polluting the waveform | Very high in modern plants | | 4 | **Ground Loops & EMI** | Multiple ground paths creating circulating currents at 50/60 Hz and radio frequencies | Almost universal in long cable runs | | 5 | **Complete Power Loss** | Blackout — handled only by UPS / battery | Rare but catastrophic | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE Part B question on this module.** | # | Formula | LaTeX | Variables & Units | KTU Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Power supply sizing** | $P_{PSU} = \sum P_{mod} + 1.25 \cdot \sum P_{I/O}$ | W | Sizing the 24 V DC PSU | | 2 | **Voltage drop on DC line** | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A}$ | V | Sensor cable sizing | | 3 | **Wire cross-section** | $A = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{V_{drop,max}}$ | mm² | Reverse of above | | 4 | **Heat dissipation in enclosure** | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$ | W | Cabinet air-conditioner sizing | | 5 | **Thermal resistance (natural conv.)** | $R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{h \cdot A_s}$ | K/W | Estimating internal temp rise | | 6 | **UPS backup time** | $t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot \eta}{P_{load}}$ | hours | Selecting battery capacity | | 7 | **Total Harmonic Distortion** | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Power-quality assessment | | 8 | **Surge energy (MOV clamp)** | $E = \int V(t) \cdot i(t)\,dt$ | J | Selecting varistor rating | | 9 | **Single-point ground resistance** | $R_g \le 1\,\Omega$ (signal), $R_g \le 5\,\Omega$ (power) | Ω | Acceptance test | | 10 | **Cable derating factor (bundled)** | $I_{allow} = I_{base} \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ | A | Multiple-cable tray | Where: - $\rho$ = resistivity of copper = **0.01724 Ω·mm²/m at 20 °C** - $h$ = heat-transfer coefficient ≈ **5–25 W/m²·K** (natural convection) - $\eta$ = inverter efficiency ≈ **0.85–0.95** - $A_s$ = effective enclosure surface area in m² ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power supply sizing** directly determines cabinet layout and the need for redundant (N+1) PSUs in Tier-1 automotive plants. - **Voltage drop calculation** is mandated by the **National Electrical Code (NEC) §210.19(A)(1)** — never more than 3 % drop on branch circuits, 5 % total feeder + branch. - **THD < 5 %** is a contract clause in most PLC procurement specifications — exceeding it voids the warranty on the CPU power supply. - **Single-point grounding** prevents **50/60 Hz ground loops** which otherwise induce **multi-volt noise** in analog I/O channels, producing reading errors of 5–10 % of span. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Methodology note:** Although "Packaging and Power System Issues" is hardware-oriented, KTU Part B (14-mark) questions routinely demand **two worked numerical problems**. The derivations below cover the three highest-probability numericals. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Supply Sizing for a Modular PLC **Statement:** A Siemens S7-1500 rack contains: 1 CPU (15 W), 1 digital-input module DI 32×24 V (6.5 W), 2 digital-output modules DO 16×24 V/0.5 A (8 W each), 1 analog-input AI 8×U/I (4.5 W), and a 24 V sensor bus powering 30 sensors at 100 mA each. Calculate the required PSU rating with **125 % safety factor** for the I/O. **Step 1 — Identify the two power domains.** A modular PLC has **two** independent power calculations: - **Backplane power** (consumed by modules through the connector) - **Sensor/actuator power** (delivered through the I/O terminals) **Step 2 — Sum the backplane power.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{backplane} &= P_{CPU} + P_{DI} + 2 \cdot P_{DO} + P_{AI} \\ &= 15 + 6.5 + 2(8) + 4.5 \\ &= 15 + 6.5 + 16 + 4.5 \\ &= 42.0\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ Each value comes from the manufacturer's datasheet, with **±5 %** tolerance. **Step 3 — Sum the sensor-load power.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{sensor} &= N \cdot I \cdot V \\ &= 30 \times 0.100\ \text{A} \times 24\ \text{V} \\ &= 72.0\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the KTU-mandated 1.25 safety factor to the I/O power only (the backplane load is already de-rated by the manufacturer).** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{PSU} &= P_{backplane} + 1.25 \cdot P_{sensor} \\ &= 42.0 + 1.25 \times 72.0 \\ &= 42.0 + 90.0 \\ &= 132.0\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Round up to the next standard commercial size.** Standard 24 V DIN-rail PSU ratings: **60, 120, 240, 480 W**. $$ \boxed{P_{PSU,\ selected} = 240\ \text{W (next size above 132 W)}} $$ > **Step logic summary:** *Sum backplane → sum field loads → apply 1.25× to field loads → add → round up.* ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Drop on a 4-Wire Sensor Cable **Statement:** A PLC DI module is connected to a 24 V DC photoelectric sensor located **85 m away**. The sensor draws **150 mA**, and the installer used **0.5 mm²** copper wire. Verify that the voltage at the sensor terminals is **≥ 19.2 V** (80 % of nominal — typical minimum for guaranteed sensor operation). **Step 1 — Recall the line-and-return voltage drop formula.** Because current flows *out* on one wire and *back* on the other, **both conductors contribute to the drop** — hence the factor of 2: $$ V_{drop} = \frac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A} $$ **Step 2 — Insert the values.** - $I = 0.150$ A - $L = 85$ m - $\rho = 0.01724\ \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$ (copper at 20 °C) - $A = 0.5$ mm² $$ \begin{aligned} V_{drop} &= \frac{2 \times 0.150 \times 85 \times 0.01724}{0.5} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 0.150 \times 85 \times 0.01724}{0.5} \\ &= \frac{4.3956}{0.5} \\ &= 8.791\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare to the supply voltage and minimum allowed.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{sensor} &= V_{supply} - V_{drop} \\ &= 24.0 - 8.791 \\ &= 15.21\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion and remedial action.** Since **15.21 V < 19.2 V**, the cable **fails the design check**. The required minimum cable cross-section is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{req} &= \frac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{V_{drop,max}} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 0.150 \times 85 \times 0.01724}{24.0 - 19.2} \\ &= \frac{4.3956}{4.8} \\ &= 0.916\ \text{mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Select the next **standard size: 1.0 mm²** (re-verify: drop = 7.48 V, V_sensor = 16.52 V → still fails). Try **1.5 mm²** (drop = 4.99 V, V_sensor = 19.01 V → still marginal). Choose **2.5 mm²** (drop = 2.99 V, V_sensor = 21.01 V → **PASS**). $$ \boxed{A_{selected} = 2.5\ \text{mm}^2 \Rightarrow V_{sensor} = 21.0\ \text{V} \ge 19.2\ \text{V} \ \checkmark} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **factor of 2** (round-trip) and use $A = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{V_{drop}}$. This **always costs 2 marks**. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — UPS Backup Time for a Safety PLC **Statement:** A safety PLC (SIL-3) loads **350 W** from a 24 V DC battery-backed UPS with two 12 V, **100 Ah** sealed lead-acid (SLA) batteries in series. The inverter efficiency is **0.88**. Calculate the backup time, accounting for the **Peukert effect** (use Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$ for SLA at the 20-hour rate). **Step 1 — Determine the DC load current at 24 V.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{DC} &= \frac{P_{load}}{V_{DC} \cdot \eta} \\ &= \frac{350}{24 \times 0.88} \\ &= \frac{350}{21.12} \\ &= 16.57\ \text{A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Peukert's equation.** The Peukert equation relates the actual usable time $t$ to a discharge rate $I$: $$ t = C \cdot \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I}\right)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{I} $$ The **rated capacity** of an SLA battery is given at the **20-hour rate** ($I_{rated} = 100/20 = 5$ A). $$ \begin{aligned} t &= 100 \cdot \left(\frac{5}{16.57}\right)^{1.25-1} \cdot \frac{1}{16.57} \\ &= 100 \cdot \left(0.3017\right)^{0.25} \cdot \frac{1}{16.57} \\ &= 100 \cdot 0.7404 \cdot 0.06035 \\ &= 4.47\ \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to minutes for industrial reporting.** $$ \boxed{t_{backup} \approx 4\ \text{h}\ 28\ \text{min}} $$ > **KTU marking guidance:** Full marks (7) require: (1) battery-series recognition, (2) inverter efficiency, (3) Peukert exponent, and (4) numerical verification. Skipping (3) costs **3 marks** because KTU boards specifically test whether students know that the "100 Ah" label is only valid at the 20-hour rate. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Power Issue → Symptom → Mitigation | Power Issue | Symptom on PLC | Diagnostic Method | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage sag | CPU brown-out, scan-time stretch | Power-quality logger (IEC 61000-4-30) | **Sag-proof UPS**, **ferro-resonant transformer** | | Surge / transient | DI/DO module failure, isolated I/O burnout | Oscilloscope with peak-detect | **MOV (Metal-Oxide Varistor)**, **TVS diode**, **SPD** | | Harmonics (> 5 % THD) | Cross-talk on analog, PSU overheating | FFT analyser, Fluke 435 | **Line reactor**, **active harmonic filter**, **K-rated transformer** | | Ground loop | 4–20 mA signal drift, RS-485 comms errors | Measure potential between grounds | **Single-point ground**, **isolated signal conditioner** | | Blackout | Process crash, recipe loss | UPS event log | **Online double-conversion UPS**, **supercapacitor backup** | | EMI / RFI | Random bit-flips, comm retries | Spectrum analyser, near-field probe | **Shielded cable (360° termination)**, **ferrite clamp** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Visualisation strategy:** Mermaid is used here to render a **functional block architecture** rather than a physical drawing, as per the protocol. Two diagrams are provided: (A) PLC packaging hierarchy, (B) power-conditioning cascade. ### 4.1 Diagram A — PLC Packaging Hierarchy and Form-Factor Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD A0[PLC Selection Start] --> A1{Total I/O Count?} A1 --> A1a["Less than 64 I/O"] --> A2a["Compact Brick PLC / Type-P
Panel Mount"] A1 --> A1b["64 to 256 I/O"] --> A2b["Modular DIN-Rail PLC / Type-D
35 mm EN 50022 Rail"] A1 --> A1c["Greater than 256 I/O"] --> A2c["Rack-Mount PLC / Type-R
19 inch IEC 60297 Chassis"] A2a --> A3{Environment?} A2b --> A3 A2c --> A3 A3 --> A3a["Indoor Clean Cabinet"] --> A4a["IP20 Sufficient"] A3 --> A3b["Factory Floor Splash"] --> A4b["IP54 Minimum"] A3 --> A3c["Wash-Down or Outdoor"] --> A4c["IP65 or IP67"] A4a --> A5[Power Input Section] A4b --> A5 A4c --> A5 A5 --> A5a["24 V DC for I/O"] A5 --> A5b["120 or 230 V AC for PSU"] A5 --> A5c["Redundant N+1 PSU Optional"] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The first branch is decided by **scale** (I/O count). - The second branch is decided by **environment** (IP rating). - The terminal block is the **power input** — the link to the next diagram. ### 4.2 Diagram B — Power-Conditioning Cascade for an Industrial PLC Cabinet ```mermaid graph LR M[Grid 415 V AC 3-Phase] --> SPD[Step 1 Surge Protective Device
Type 1 or 2 per IEC 61643-11] SPD --> ISO[Step 2 Isolation Transformer
K-Factor 13 for VFD Harmonics] ISO --> VREG[Step 3 Automatic Voltage Stabiliser
Sag and Swell Correction] VREG --> FILTER[Step 4 EMI RFI Line Filter
Common Mode and Differential Mode] FILTER --> UPS[Step 5 Online Double Conversion UPS
Bypass Switch Included] UPS --> PSU24[Step 6 24 V DC Power Supply
Redundant N+1 Configuration] PSU24 --> PLC[Step 7 PLC CPU and Modules] PSU24 --> IO[Step 8 Field Sensors and Actuators] PLC --- GND[Single Point Ground Star Topology
R less than 1 Ohm for Signal Ground] IO --- GND UPS -.Battery.-> BAT[Sealed Lead Acid 12 V 100 Ah x 2] ``` **Reading the cascade:** Each block performs a **single, defined function**. The order is non-negotiable: 1. **SPD first** — shunts high-energy lightning/switching surges to ground *before* they reach the transformer. 2. **Isolation transformer second** — breaks the galvanic path (kills ground loops) and absorbs common-mode noise. 3. **Voltage stabiliser third** — corrects slow sags/swells that the UPS may be slow to react to. 4. **EMI filter fourth** — removes high-frequency noise. 5. **UPS fifth** — provides ride-through and black-start capability. 6. **24 V PSU sixth** — the final, clean DC bus. 7. **Single-point ground (bottom)** — every block returns to **one** PE bar at the cabinet entry. > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Placing the UPS *before* the surge protector. This is wrong — the UPS is expensive and the SPD is the sacrificial element. Place SPD **upstream** of UPS. ### 4.3 Diagram C — IP-Code Decision Tree for PLC Enclosure Selection ```mermaid graph TD Q1[What is the surrounding medium?] --> Q1a[Air with normal dust] Q1a --> R1a[IP5x family] Q1 --> Q1b[Liquid spray or wash-down] Q1b --> R1b[IPx5 or IPx6] Q1 --> Q1c[Temporary submersion] Q1c --> R1c[IPx7] Q1 --> Q1d[Continuous submersion] Q1d --> R1d[IPx8] Q2[What solid particle exposure?] --> Q2a[No requirement, finger-safe only] Q2a --> R2a[IP2x] Q2 --> Q2b[Dust ingress must be limited] Q2b --> R2b[IP5x] Q2 --> Q2c[Dust must be excluded entirely] Q2c --> R2c[IP6x] R1a --> FINAL[Example IP54 means dust protected and splash proof] R1b --> FINAL R2a --> FINAL R2b --> FINAL R2c --> FINAL R1c --> FINAL R1d --> FINAL ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark distribution for this module (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks; Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks; **Total for module: 20 marks** in the ESE. --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** **Explain the IP65 rating with reference to PLC packaging.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The IP code is defined by **IEC 60529**. The "IP" stands for **Ingress Protection**. - The **first digit (6)** indicates **dust-tight** protection — no ingress of dust is permitted, and the enclosure is sealed against contact with moving or energised parts. - The **second digit (5)** indicates protection against **water jets** projected by a **6.3 mm nozzle** from any direction — the water shall have no harmful effect. For PLC applications, **IP65 is the minimum rating for wash-down areas** such as food and beverage, pharmaceutical, and outdoor installations. It is achieved through **gasketed covers, captive screws, sealed cable glands (compression type, IP68-rated), and sealed ventilation plugs (Gore-Tex vents for breathability)**. The advantage over IP54 is that the enclosure can be hosed down without internal condensation damage. > **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for IEC 60529 reference, 1 mark for digit-by-digit explanation, 1 mark for industrial application. #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** **Why is single-point grounding mandatory in a PLC installation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Single-point grounding (also called **star-ground topology**) is mandatory because it **eliminates ground loops** — closed conductive paths in which **50/60 Hz circulating currents** and high-frequency noise currents are induced by the **potential difference** between two different ground points (e.g., the cabinet ground bar and the motor-frame ground, which can differ by **0.5–3 V** under load). If multiple ground paths exist, the resulting circulating current **flows through the analog I/O cable shield**, raising the **common-mode voltage** at the PLC input beyond the **±15 V CM range** of the input stage. The result is **systematic reading errors** (typically 2–5 % of span on 4–20 mA loops) and **intermittent RS-485 communication failures** due to exceeding the **±7 V** transceiver common-mode limit. The remedy is to bond **all PLC signal grounds to one PE bar at one location**, with **earth-electrode resistance ≤ 1 Ω** for the signal ground (≤ 5 Ω for the power ground per IS 3043). > **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for defining ground loop, 1 mark for the failure mechanism, 1 mark for the single-point remedy with resistance value. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** One 14-mark question from this module with **internal choice** (two alternatives; answer any ONE). Each alternative has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) is typically a descriptive/analytical question, sub-part (b) is a numerical/design problem. --- ### **Question A — Option 1** *(Packaging focus)* #### **(a)** [7 marks] **Explain the different types of PLC packaging with neat sketches, indicating the standard mounting rails used for each. Discuss the role of environmental specifications (IP rating, temperature, humidity) in selecting a PLC package.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** PLC packaging defines the **mechanical, electrical, and environmental envelope** of the controller. The choice of package is governed by the **I/O count**, the **physical installation environment**, and the **expected service life** under industrial stress. **Types of PLC Packaging (4 marks):** 1. **Rack / Chassis Mount PLC (Type-R):** - Used in **centralized architectures** with very large I/O counts (> 256 points). - Mounts in a **19-inch sub-rack** as per **IEC 60297**. - Modules (CPU, I/O, communication) plug vertically into a common **backplane** that carries power and data. - Examples: Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-400. - **Advantage:** hot-swappable modules, easy expansion; **disadvantage:** higher cost, larger footprint. 2. **DIN-Rail Mount PLC (Type-D):** - Industry workhorse, snaps onto a **35 mm top-hat rail (EN 50022)** or **32 mm G-rail (EN 50035)**. - **Modular** — CPU, I/O, and PSU are separate units wired by removable terminal blocks. - Examples: Siemens S7-1200/1500, Schneider M340, ABB AC500. - Spring-clamp terminals (vibration-proof) and **PLCs with IP20 finger-safe** rating are standard. 3. **Panel Mount / Wall Mount PLC (Type-P):** - All-in-one brick with **integrated I/O**, displays, and keypads. - Mounts through a **rectangular cut-out** in the control panel door. - IP65 / NEMA 4X front face is common — survives wash-down. - Examples: Siemens LOGO!, Mitsubishi Alpha, Schneider Zelio. 4. **Open-Frame / Board-Level PLC (Type-O):** - Bare PCB intended for **OEM embedding** inside a customer-built enclosure. - Used in cost-sensitive, high-volume applications (vending machines, lifts, HVAC). **Environmental Specifications (2 marks):** - **IP Rating (IEC 60529):** IP20 for cabinet internals, IP54/65 for floor-level cabinets or wet zones. The first digit is solid-particle protection (6 = dust-tight), the second is water protection (5 = jet-proof). - **Operating Temperature:** Standard industrial range is **0 to +60 °C**; extended range reaches **−40 to +85 °C** for outdoor cabinets in Kerala's monsoon-prone environment. - **Relative Humidity:** **5 to 95 % non-condensing** — a critical specification in humid coastal states. Condensation inside the cabinet is prevented by **anti-condensation heaters (10–30 W)** and **Gore-Tex IP67 vents** that allow moisture vapor to escape but block liquid water. - **Vibration & Shock:** Per **IEC 60068-2-6 (2 g, 10–500 Hz)** and **IEC 60068-2-27 (15 g, 11 ms)** — achieved through **spring-clamp terminals, locking connectors, and captive screws**. > **Mark split:** 1 (intro) + 4 (four types with standards) + 2 (environmental specs). #### **(b)** [7 marks] **A PLC panel is installed in a CNC machine shop. The ambient temperature is 50 °C, and the panel houses 8 I/O modules dissipating 4 W each, plus a CPU dissipating 18 W, in a steel cabinet of dimensions 800 mm (H) × 600 mm (W) × 300 mm (D). Estimate the internal temperature rise and determine whether a cooling fan is required. Assume $h = 7\ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}$ (natural convection, vertical steel, unpolished).** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Total internal heat dissipation (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{diss} &= 8 \times 4 + 18 \\ &= 32 + 18 \\ &= 50\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Effective surface area of the cabinet (1 mark):** The effective area is the **total surface area minus the bottom** (heat rises — bottom contributes negligibly). $$ \begin{aligned} A_s &= 2 \times (H \times W) + 2 \times (H \times D) + W \times D \\ &= 2 \times (0.8 \times 0.6) + 2 \times (0.8 \times 0.3) + (0.6 \times 0.3) \\ &= 2 \times 0.48 + 2 \times 0.24 + 0.18 \\ &= 0.96 + 0.48 + 0.18 \\ &= 1.62\ \text{m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ (Subtracting the bottom, $A_s = 1.62 - 0.18 = 1.44\ \text{m}^2$.) **Step 3 — Thermal resistance of the enclosure (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{th} &= \frac{1}{h \cdot A_s} \\ &= \frac{1}{7 \times 1.44} \\ &= \frac{1}{10.08} \\ &= 0.0992\ \text{K/W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Steady-state temperature rise (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= P_{diss} \times R_{th} \\ &= 50 \times 0.0992 \\ &= 4.96\ \text{K} \approx 5.0\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Internal temperature (1 mark):** $$ T_{internal} = T_{ambient} + \Delta T = 50 + 5.0 = 55.0\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 6 — Compare with the PLC rating and conclude (2 marks):** - Standard industrial PLC rating: **0 to +60 °C** operating. - Computed internal temperature: **55 °C**, which is **5 °C below** the limit. **Conclusion:** A natural-convection design is **just adequate**, but with **zero safety margin**. In a CNC shop, additional heat sources include the **spindle-drive VFD (≈ 200 W)** and **hydraulic power pack (≈ 100 W)**. Including these, the panel would exceed 70 °C and the **PLC warranty would be voided**. **Recommendation:** Install a **cabinet cooling fan** (typically 100–150 m³/h, 25 W) or, preferably, a **closed-loop cabinet air-conditioner** (Peltier or compressor type, 300–500 W cooling capacity). A simple filter fan upgrade extends component life by **3–5×** in tropical climates like Kerala. > **Mark split:** 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 7 marks. --- ### **Question B — Option 2** *(Power System focus)* #### **(a)** [7 marks] **Explain the major power-system issues encountered in a PLC installation. With the help of a block diagram, describe how each issue is mitigated in a properly designed industrial power-conditioning cascade.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** PLC electronics are **sensitive semiconductor devices** rated for ±10 % voltage tolerance and 5 % THD. Industrial mains power, however, is subject to **sags, surges, harmonics, EMI, and complete blackouts**. A **power-conditioning cascade** mitigates each issue in a defined order. **Major Power-System Issues and Mitigations (5 marks):** 1. **Voltage Sag (Dip):** - **Cause:** Large motor starting, grid faults. RMS drops to 10–90 % for 0.5 cycle – 1 min. - **Mitigation:** **Automatic Voltage Stabiliser (AVS)** with ±1 % regulation, followed by an **online double-conversion UPS** with transfer time < 4 ms (one-quarter cycle at 50 Hz). 2. **Surge / Transient:** - **Cause:** Lightning, capacitor-bank switching, contactor or solenoid dropout (back-EMF of 2–4 kV). - **Mitigation:** **Surge Protective Device (SPD)** — Type 1 (10/350 µs, 25 kA) at the building entry; Type 2 (8/20 µs, 40 kA) inside the cabinet. Clamping devices include **MOV (Metal-Oxide Varistor)**, **TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diode**, and **gas-discharge tube**. 3. **Harmonic Distortion (THD > 5 %):** - **Cause:** Variable-Frequency Drives (VFDs), switched-mode power supplies, LED drivers. The 5th and 7th harmonics are the most troublesome. - **Mitigation:** **K-rated transformer (K-factor 13)** for non-linear loads, **line reactor (3–5 % impedance)** at the VFD input, and **active harmonic filter (AHF)** to cancel harmonics in real time. 4. **EMI / RFI Noise:** - **Cause:** VFDs, welding machines, walkie-talkies near I/O cables. - **Mitigation:** **EMI/RFI line filter** (common-mode and differential-mode) at the cabinet input, **shielded twisted-pair (STP) cables** for analog and communication, **360° shield termination** at both ends, **ferrite clamp cores** on every cable leaving the panel. 5. **Complete Blackout:** - **Cause:** Grid failure, breaker trip. - **Mitigation:** **Online double-conversion UPS** with **sealed lead-acid (SLA) or lithium-iron-phosphate (LiFePO₄) battery bank** sized for the required autonomy (typically 15–30 min for orderly shutdown, 4 h for safety PLCs). 6. **Ground Loop and Improper Grounding:** - **Cause:** Multiple bonding points causing circulating currents. - **Mitigation:** **Single-point (star) grounding** at one PE bar inside the cabinet; **isolation transformer** to break the galvanic path; **optical isolation** for analog signals. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** The student is expected to draw a cascade showing: **Mains → SPD → Isolation Transformer → AVS → EMI Filter → UPS → 24 V DC PSU → PLC**. (Refer to Diagram B in Section 4 above for the complete schematic.) **Real-World Reference (bonus mark, optional):** A typical Tier-1 automotive plant uses **N+1 redundant PSUs** and a **rotary UPS (DRUPS)** for the safety PLC to ride through 30-second complete blackouts. > **Mark split:** 1 (intro) + 5 (six issues with mitigations) + 1 (cascade diagram). #### **(b)** [7 marks] **A 24 V DC PLC system draws a continuous current of 8.5 A from its PSU. The plant uses a 24 V battery-backed UPS with two 12 V, 60 Ah batteries in series. The inverter efficiency is 0.85. Calculate the backup time. If the plant engineer wants a minimum 1-hour backup, what is the minimum battery capacity required (at the 20-hour rate, Peukert exponent n = 1.25)?** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Determine the DC current drawn from the 24 V battery (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{DC} &= \frac{P_{load}}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC,load}}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta} \\ &= \frac{24 \times 8.5}{24 \times 0.85} \\ &= \frac{8.5}{0.85} \\ &= 10.0\ \text{A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Peukert's equation for the actual backup time (2 marks):** For SLA at 20-hour rate, $I_{rated} = 60/20 = 3$ A. $$ \begin{aligned} t_{actual} &= C \cdot \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I_{DC}}\right)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{I_{DC}} \\ &= 60 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{10}\right)^{0.25} \cdot \frac{1}{10} \\ &= 60 \cdot (0.3)^{0.25} \cdot 0.1 \\ &= 60 \cdot 0.7401 \cdot 0.1 \\ &= 4.44\ \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Reverse calculation for 1-hour target (2 marks):** For $t_{target} = 1$ h, we need a battery capacity $C_{new}$ such that: $$ t = \frac{C_{new} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{new}/20}{I_{DC}}\right)^{n-1}}{I_{DC}} = 1 $$ Substituting $I_{DC} = 10$ A, $n - 1 = 0.25$: $$ 1 = \frac{C_{new}}{10} \cdot \left(\frac{C_{new}}{200}\right)^{0.25} $$ Let $x = C_{new}$: $$ 10 = x \cdot \left(\frac{x}{200}\right)^{0.25} $$ **Step 4 — Solve iteratively (1 mark):** - Try $x = 80$ Ah: $80 \cdot (80/200)^{0.25} = 80 \cdot (0.4)^{0.25} = 80 \cdot 0.795 = 63.6$ — too high. - Try $x = 70$ Ah: $70 \cdot (0.35)^{0.25} = 70 \cdot 0.770 = 53.9$ — too high. - Try $x = 60$ Ah: $60 \cdot (0.30)^{0.25} = 60 \cdot 0.740 = 44.4$ — still > 10 h, but we want t = 1 h. Wait — the equation is wrong; reformulate. **Re-formulation (the correct way, 1 mark):** Re-arrange the Peukert equation for capacity: $$ C_{new} = I_{DC} \cdot t \cdot \left(\frac{I_{DC}}{I_{rated, new}}\right)^{n-1} $$ Since $I_{rated, new} = C_{new}/20$, this is a transcendental equation. Solve by iteration with **simple approximation** (KTU-level): The **Peukert-corrected runtime** for a given capacity at a given load is: $$ t = \frac{C}{I_{DC}} \cdot \left(\frac{C/20}{I_{DC}}\right)^{n-1} $$ Iterate for $t = 1$ h at $I_{DC} = 10$ A: - Try $C = 14$ Ah: $t = 14/10 \cdot (14/200)^{0.25} = 1.4 \cdot (0.07)^{0.25} = 1.4 \cdot 0.514 = 0.72$ h — too low. - Try $C = 18$ Ah: $t = 1.8 \cdot (0.09)^{0.25} = 1.8 \cdot 0.549 = 0.99$ h — close. - Try $C = 20$ Ah: $t = 2.0 \cdot (0.10)^{0.25} = 2.0 \cdot 0.562 = 1.12$ h — adequate. **Final answer:** $C_{min} \approx 20$ Ah. **Step 5 — Engineering conclusion (1 mark):** Select the next standard size: **24 Ah** (2 × 12 V, 24 Ah in series). The actual backup will be **≈ 1.4 h**, giving a comfortable safety margin. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the Peukert correction (n = 1.25)** — this is the most common 2-mark loss. Many students compute the "textbook" backup of $60/10 = 6$ h, which is the **20-hour-rate** value and is only correct if the load current equals 3 A. The question clearly states 8.5 A. > 2. **Inverter efficiency missing** — the load current on the battery is **not** equal to the load current on the 24 V bus. > 3. **Series battery connection** — the two 12 V batteries in series give **24 V**, **60 Ah** (not 120 Ah — voltage adds, capacity stays the same). > 4. **Unit confusion** — backup time must be expressed in **hours** when using the Peukert equation in this form. > **Mark split:** 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 7 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density KTU revision bullets — read once, retain forever.** - **Packaging is not cosmetic.** It is the **first line of defence** against dust, water, heat, vibration, and EMI. - **Three form factors:** Rack (19″), DIN-rail (35 mm EN 50022), Panel mount. Each corresponds to a **scale of installation** (large / distributed / compact). - **IP code** = International Protection per **IEC 60529**. **First digit = solids (max 6), second digit = liquids (max 9).** - **PLC power system issues** are **sag, surge, harmonics, EMI, blackout, ground loop** — remember the acronym **"SHE-BG"** for the exam. - **Power cascade order is fixed:** SPD → Isolation Transformer → AVS → EMI Filter → UPS → 24 V DC PSU → PLC. **Do not reorder.** - **Single-point (star) grounding** prevents 50/60 Hz ground loops. Earth resistance ≤ **1 Ω** (signal), ≤ **5 Ω** (power) per IS 3043. - **Voltage drop formula uses factor 2** for round-trip current — this is a **classic 2-mark KTU trap**. - **Power supply sizing** uses a **1.25 safety factor** on the field I/O load (not the backplane load). - **Peukert exponent** $n$ is **1.25** for SLA batteries at the 20-hour rate — applies to UPS sizing. - **Surge protection uses MOVs / TVS diodes** — clamping voltage must be **~1.4 × nominal** (e.g., 385 V MOV for 230 V AC). - **Harmonics** are addressed with **K-rated transformers, line reactors, and active harmonic filters** (THD target < 5 %). - **Kerala-specific concern:** ambient temperature in factory floors often reaches **45–50 °C**; design with a **5–10 K safety margin** and consider **closed-loop cabinet cooling** in CNC/foundry shops. - **Backup time for safety PLCs (SIL-3)** must support **safe shutdown** — typically 30 min minimum, often 4 h in process plants. - **EMC compliance is a legal requirement** under **IEC 61131-2** for industrial PLCs — never waive the line filter to save cost. > **End of Module 4 — Packaging and Power System Issues Notes**
